addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 feat: decompose output config into register verbs —
     `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `set-ascii-hz` (reg 7),
     `get/set-bin` (reg 75), and `set-bin-hz` (the reg-75
     divisor), all as `key=value` tokens. Drops `get-hz`/`set-hz`.
     Keeps the legacy configure/measure `bench` as a parallel path
     until -5. (done)
   - 0.3.0-5 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. Now exercisable via the -4 verbs (`set-bin`
     then `bench`). (done — impl landed; an intermittent
     cold-open 0/0 remains, tracked as Todo #1)
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     merge `set-bin`+`set-bin-hz` in one word into a single reg-75
     write.
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;

message.content:
Actually last night we were working on reliability and had trouble properly getting ascii and bin working streams working at the same time. ATM I just powered the rpi5 we're on and the vn-100 it's connected to and executed rw-vn100 4 times in the default configuration without issue. That's not definitive it would always work. Two significant differences from last night this is baud is 115200 instead of 921600 and only the ascii stream, here are the results:

content.text:
Thanks — that's a clean, encouraging result, and you're right to flag it as suggestive rather than definitive. A few thoughts:

**The two differences are confounded.** You changed *baud* (921600 → 115200) and *stream count* (ASCII+binary → ASCII-only) at the same time, so these 4 runs can't tell us which one bought the reliability. Last night's trouble was specifically "ascii and bin at the same time" at 921600 — so the most likely culprits are either the higher data rate stressing the cold-open buffer, or the binary-stream config itself (the reg-75 serial-port-on-TTL stuff we fixed earlier). This run exercises neither.

**Run 1 is the meaningful one for the zero-parse bug.** The intermittent `ASCII: none / Binary: none` is cold-open sensitive — it bites the *first* open after power-up. You powered fresh and your 14:50:12 run was the genuine cold open, and it parsed fine. Runs 2–4 are warm and matter less. So: one clean cold open at 115200 ASCII-only. Good data point, but n=1 for the case that actually matters.

**The numbers check out.** 39.5–39.8 Hz VNYMR, ~122 B/msg, 49 kbit/s = 42% of the link. That's the configured 40 Hz ASCII with comfortable headroom — no back-pressure, which is consistent with no parse trouble.

If you want to actually pin down what fixed reliability, vary one knob at a time, each from a cold power-cycle (since cold-open is the sensitive moment):

- **Hold baud, add binary back** (115200, ASCII+bin) — tests whether "both streams" is the trigger independent of baud.
- **Hold stream-count, raise baud** (921600, ASCII-only) — tests whether 921600 alone destabilizes the cold open.

Whichever reproduces the zero-parse points at the real variable, and that's the one to instrument with the env-gated raw dump (Todo #1).

What's the goal this morning — chase down the zero-parse, or get both streams reliable at 115200 first since that's the proven path toward the fc.py 200 Hz work?

message.content: I agree, but this was simply the state on power on, so it's reasonble to test. One other thing I think we might want to do, but probably after we reproduce the problem, is add a mode where we collect the data during a run so that we can actually capture the bad data. I'm thinking we capture it in memory not try to stream it to a file in real time so as to minimize changes. It fails right away so I'm hoping/assuming we have enough RAM to run a few minutes. Again, I'm thinking we reporduce the issue first, which means enabling binary as the second stream. Thoughts

content.text:
Both ideas are sound, and they line up well with the existing plan. Thoughts:

**In-memory capture — RAM is a non-issue, and in-memory is the *right* call for a subtler reason than just "fewer changes."** Streaming to a file mid-loop adds write syscalls inside the read path, which perturbs the very timing we're trying to characterize — it could mask or shift the bug. A growing `Vec<u8>` (dump once at exit) keeps the hot loop clean. On the numbers: 115200 maxes at ~11.5 kB/s, so 5 minutes is ~3.5 MB; even at 921600 fully saturated it's ~90 kB/s → ~27 MB for 5 min. Your RPi5 has GBs — you could capture an hour and not notice. So drop any worry about RAM; capture liberally.

Worth naming explicitly: **this capture mode *is* Todo #1's "env-gated raw dump" [[8]]**, just realized as collect-in-RAM-then-dump rather than live-stream. Same artifact (a raw byte log you can diff against `test-data/both-streams.bin`), better mechanism. So building it isn't a detour — it's the instrument the bug-hunt already called for. I'd gate it on an env var (e.g. `RW_VN100_CAPTURE=path`) so the normal path is byte-for-byte unchanged. And since the bug bites at cold open, timestamping the chunks (`Instant` per read) would let us see exactly *when* in the first second things go wrong.

**Reproduction by enabling binary — agree it's the next step, but two cautions so we reproduce the *right* failure:**

1. **Don't overflow the link, or you'll get a saturation drop masquerading as the cold-open bug.** At 115200 you're already at 42% with 40 Hz ASCII. If binary goes on at a high rate (and the 200 Hz fc.py goal tempts that), ASCII+binary can exceed 115.2 kbit/s — that's a *bandwidth* failure, a different beast from the intermittent cold-open zero-parse. To target the cold-open bug specifically, pick binary rates that comfortably fit under the link; to reproduce last night's high-baud condition, go to 921600 where both streams have headroom.

2. **Enabling binary right now means a port reopen.** The single-connection step (`0.3.0-6`) isn't built yet, so it's `set-bin …` (reg 75) as one command, then `bench` as another — and each reopen is itself a wedge die-roll per the notes. That's fine for reproducing, but keep it in mind: if the failure shows up *between* the two commands, it may be the reopen, not the stream config.

So my suggested order: from the current clean 115200 state, add binary at a rate that fits the link and re-run the cold-open test (one knob changed). If it reproduces — baud wasn't needed, and we instrument right there. If it doesn't, go to 921600 with both streams to match last night. Want me to work out concrete `set-bin`/`set-bin-hz` values that keep us under 115.2 kbit/s before you try it?

lastPrompt: I agree, but this was simply the state on power on, so it's reasonble to test. One other thing I think we might want to do, but probably after we reproduce the problem, is add a mode where we collect…

lastPrompt: yes, work out the set-bin values under 115.2 kbit/s

input.description: Confirm CRC size and default baud

content.text:
Got the numbers I need. The binary frame is `payload + 6` bytes on the wire (sync 1 + groups 1 + mask 2 + CRC 2 = 6 of overhead), and the tool counts wire throughput at 10 bits/byte (8N1), matching your `4850 B/s = 49 kbit/s`. Field payload sizes: time 8, ypr 12, quat 16, gyro 12, accel 12, imu 24, magpres 20.

**Budget.** Link = 11,520 B/s. ASCII at 40 Hz eats ~4,855 B/s (49 kbit/s, 42%), leaving ~6,665 B/s. I'll keep total ≤ ~90% to leave UART jitter headroom (no flow control), so the binary budget is ~5,500 B/s.

**`set-bin=time,accel,gyro`** (payload 32 → 38 B/frame), with ASCII still at 40 Hz:

| Binary Hz | divisor | binary B/s | total B/s | % of 115.2 | verdict |
|----------|---------|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| 50  | 16 | 1,900 | 6,755 | 59% | comfortable |
| **100** | **8** | **3,800** | **8,655** | **75%** | **recommended** |
| 200 | 4 | 7,600 | 12,455 | 108% | ❌ overflows |
| 400 | 2 | 15,200 | — | — | ❌ overflows |

200 Hz is off the table at 115200 with a 38 B frame — that would be a *saturation* drop, exactly the false positive we want to avoid conflating with the cold-open bug.

**Recommended sequence** (each a separate invocation, since the single-connection `-6` step isn't built yet):

```
rw-vn100 set-bin=time,accel,gyro --baud 115200
rw-vn100 set-bin-hz=100 --baud 115200
rw-vn100 bench --baud 115200 2
```

Order matters: `set-bin` first enables the mask (a never-configured output defaults to divisor 20 / 40 Hz and warns), then `set-bin-hz=100` overrides the rate. Expected `bench`: ASCII ~40 Hz + Binary ~100 Hz, ~75% link — a genuine dual stream with clear headroom, so any `ASCII: none / Binary: none` is the real cold-open bug, not back-pressure.

Two notes:
- `set-*` **writes flash**, so this config persists across power cycles. To clear binary later: `rw-vn100 set-bin=off`.
- If you'd rather push toward the fc.py 200 Hz rate while reproducing, you *can* hit 200 Hz binary at 115200 only with a lean frame — e.g. `set-bin=ypr` (18 B) @ 200 Hz = 3,600 B/s → total 73%, or `set-bin=time,gyro` (26 B) @ 200 Hz = 5,200 B/s → total 87% (tight; drop ASCII to 20 Hz via `set-ascii-hz=20` to relax it). But for a clean first reproduction I'd stick with the 100 Hz `time,accel,gyro` row.

Want to run the recommended sequence, or do you want to drive it from the device side yourself and just report back?

message.content: I don't think set-* writes flash as we did set's last night and they did not survive the power cycle, please check the code.

content.text:
You're right, and I was wrong. The code shows the opposite of what I claimed:

- **`Command::SetBin`** (lib.rs:254) and **`Command::SetBinHz`** (lib.rs:285) only call `save_to_flash(...)` **`if persist`** — i.e. only when you pass `--persist`.
- The bare `VNWRG,75,...` write goes to the **volatile register**. VN-100 registers are RAM-backed and revert on power cycle unless explicitly committed to flash (a separate WNV/`VNWNV` command, which is what `save_to_flash` issues).

So without `--persist`, `set-bin` / `set-bin-hz` are exactly volatile — which matches what you saw last night: the configs didn't survive the power cycle. My "set-* writes flash" claim was wrong.

The source of my error: the **`--help` text itself says it writes flash**. From cli.rs:171:

```
"  `set-*` writes flash. Bare `set-bin` re-enables the current mask.\n"
```

That line is misleading — it states an unconditional flash write, but the code only writes flash on `--persist`. So there's a real **doc/behavior mismatch in the help text** worth fixing (the help should say something like "`set-*` writes the volatile register; add `--persist` to also save to flash").

Net effect on the reproduction plan: **good news, actually** — the recommended sequence

```
rw-vn100 set-bin=time,accel,gyro --baud 115200
rw-vn100 set-bin-hz=100 --baud 115200
rw-vn100 bench --baud 115200 2
```

leaves flash untouched, so a power cycle returns the device to ASCII-only. No `set-bin=off` cleanup needed — just power-cycle. And it means each cold-open test starts from the same flash-default state, which is exactly what we want for the cold-open bug hunt.

Want me to note that help-text mismatch as a Todo/bug while we're here, or leave it until after the reproduction work?

message.content: note that help-text mismatch as a bug

content.text: I'll check the bugs list format and the exact help-text location before adding the entry.

content.content:
1	# Bugs
2	
3	This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
4	defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
5	describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.
6	
7	Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
8	permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
9	never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
10	anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
11	title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.
12	
13	Next Issue #: 3
14	
15	## Bugs
16	
17	### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART
18	
19	A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rw-vn100` invocation opening
20	the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
21	afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
22	by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
23	per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
24	baud.
25	
26	The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
27	command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
28	connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
29	**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.
30	
31	- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
32	  between switches):
33	    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
34	      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
35	    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
36	      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
37	    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
38	      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
39	      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
40	- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
41	  baud, explicit `--baud`):
42	    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
43	      clean, 40.3 Hz.
44	    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
45	    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
46	      40.0 Hz.
47	    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
48	      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
49	      115200 / 40 Hz.
50	- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
51	  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
52	- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
53	  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
54	  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
55	  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
56	  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
57	- The bench link is RS-232: a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C USB-C to
58	  RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps) into the
59	  VN-100's RS-232 port. 921600 is ~2× that rating. We think the
60	  cause is RS-232 slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's
61	  transceiver and on the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver,
62	  neither swappable — mis-framing bytes into the device at high
63	  baud, which its firmware reacts to by wedging. It is *not* an
64	  FTDI control-line transient: no handshake lines are wired. Full
65	  analysis: chores-01 [[1]].
66	- This is an RS-232-rig finding. The RPi5 flight target uses a
67	  direct 3.3 V TTL UART with no RS-232 transceivers in the path,
68	  so the wedge must be retested there before it is treated as a
69	  VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.
70	
71	**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
72	that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
73	ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
74	power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
75	200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
76	higher device baud.
77	
78	### Issue #2 — `read_reply` closure parameter named `matches` collides with the `matches!` macro
79	
80	In `read_reply` (`src/main.rs`), the predicate closure parameter is
81	named `matches`, while the same function body uses the std `matches!`
82	macro to classify a read error kind. Two unrelated things share the
83	name in one scope:
84	
85	- `matches!(e.kind(), ErrorKind::TimedOut | ErrorKind::WouldBlock)` —
86	  the standard-library macro (note the `!`), a pattern test on the
87	  I/O error.
88	- `matches(&candidate)` — the caller-supplied `FnMut(&str) -> bool`
89	  closure that decides whether an assembled reply line is the one
90	  we're waiting for. It is **not** an exact-equality test: every
91	  call site passes a `starts_with` *prefix* predicate (e.g.
92	  `|l| l.starts_with("$VNWRG,75")`), and `transact` further OR's in
93	  a `$VNERR` prefix so a device-error reply also terminates the read
94	  (`read_reply(port, deadline, |l| accept(l) || l.starts_with("$VNERR"))`).
95	  `read_reply` slices `candidate` from the last `$` before testing,
96	  so the prefix is anchored to the real reply, not stray leading
97	  bytes.
98	
99	The closure threads through `transact_retry -> transact -> read_reply`
100	as `impl Fn(&str) -> bool`. A reader scanning `read_reply` sees the
101	`matches!(...)` macro first and reasonably assumes the later
102	`matches(...)` is the same thing, when the actual closure invocation
103	is the prefix-based line-acceptance test.
104	
105	- Proposed fix: rename the parameter (and its doc-comment mention) to
106	  `is_acceptable` — it is a function parameter and call sites are
107	  unaffected (the closure is passed positionally).
108	- Pure readability defect: behavior is correct; the name collision
109	  costs reader time and invites misreading.
110	
111	**Cost / why it matters:** no runtime impact, but `read_reply` is the
112	core of every command's reply path, so the confusion tax is paid by
113	anyone tracing how `GetHZ` (or any transact) reads a reply.
114	
115	# References
116	
117	[1]: chores/chores-01.md#rs-232-link-analysis
118

file.content:
# Bugs

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.

Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.

Next Issue #: 3

## Bugs

### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART

A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rw-vn100` invocation opening
the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
baud.

The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.

- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
  between switches):
    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
  baud, explicit `--baud`):
    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
      clean, 40.3 Hz.
    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
      40.0 Hz.
    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
      115200 / 40 Hz.
- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
- The bench link is RS-232: a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C USB-C to
  RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps) into the
  VN-100's RS-232 port. 921600 is ~2× that rating. We think the
  cause is RS-232 slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's
  transceiver and on the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver,
  neither swappable — mis-framing bytes into the device at high
  baud, which its firmware reacts to by wedging. It is *not* an
  FTDI control-line transient: no handshake lines are wired. Full
  analysis: chores-01 [[1]].
- This is an RS-232-rig finding. The RPi5 flight target uses a
  direct 3.3 V TTL UART with no RS-232 transceivers in the path,
  so the wedge must be retested there before it is treated as a
  VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.

**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
higher device baud.

### Issue #2 — `read_reply` closure parameter named `matches` collides with the `matches!` macro

In `read_reply` (`src/main.rs`), the predicate closure parameter is
named `matches`, while the same function body uses the std `matches!`
macro to classify a read error kind. Two unrelated things share the
name in one scope:

- `matches!(e.kind(), ErrorKind::TimedOut | ErrorKind::WouldBlock)` —
  the standard-library macro (note the `!`), a pattern test on the
  I/O error.
- `matches(&candidate)` — the caller-supplied `FnMut(&str) -> bool`
  closure that decides whether an assembled reply line is the one
  we're waiting for. It is **not** an exact-equality test: every
  call site passes a `starts_with` *prefix* predicate (e.g.
  `|l| l.starts_with("$VNWRG,75")`), and `transact` further OR's in
  a `$VNERR` prefix so a device-error reply also terminates the read
  (`read_reply(port, deadline, |l| accept(l) || l.starts_with("$VNERR"))`).
  `read_reply` slices `candidate` from the last `$` before testing,
  so the prefix is anchored to the real reply, not stray leading
  bytes.

The closure threads through `transact_retry -> transact -> read_reply`
as `impl Fn(&str) -> bool`. A reader scanning `read_reply` sees the
`matches!(...)` macro first and reasonably assumes the later
`matches(...)` is the same thing, when the actual closure invocation
is the prefix-based line-acceptance test.

- Proposed fix: rename the parameter (and its doc-comment mention) to
  `is_acceptable` — it is a function parameter and call sites are
  unaffected (the closure is passed positionally).
- Pure readability defect: behavior is correct; the name collision
  costs reader time and invites misreading.

**Cost / why it matters:** no runtime impact, but `read_reply` is the
core of every command's reply path, so the confusion tax is paid by
anyone tracing how `GetHZ` (or any transact) reads a reply.

# References

[1]: chores/chores-01.md#rs-232-link-analysis

content.text:
Before writing the entry I re-read the full help string in context — and there's actually **no mismatch**. I misread it last turn by quoting line 171 in isolation. The sentence spans two `push_str` lines (cli.rs:170–171), and rendered it reads:

> Output config — each verb owns one register; **`--persist` on a `set-*` writes flash.** Bare `set-bin` re-enables the current mask.

The subject is `--persist`, not `set-*`. So the help text says exactly what the code does: a `set-*` writes flash *only when you add `--persist`*. That matches lib.rs:254 / lib.rs:285 (`if persist { save_to_flash(...) }`).

My earlier quote chopped off the leading "`--persist` on a" because the line break split the sentence, which made it look like an unconditional "set-* writes flash." That was my reading error, not a code/doc bug.

So I'd recommend **not** filing this — there's nothing to fix. The substantive conclusion from before still holds and is the part that matters: without `--persist`, `set-bin`/`set-bin-hz` are volatile and revert on power cycle, which is what you observed last night.

That said — the wrapping clearly tripped *me*, and a human skimming `--help` could hit the same ambiguity, since the rendered output also breaks the line mid-sentence. If you want, I could file a much narrower **readability** nit (reword so `--persist`'s conditional role survives the line wrap, e.g. "`set-*` verbs write the volatile register; add `--persist` to also save to flash") — but that's a wording polish, not a correctness bug. Your call: file the readability nit, or drop it?

Renders cleanly — the two-sentence paragraph now reads as one unit, with a blank line separating it from the `get-ascii` row.

On the feedback itself: noted, and it's a fair hit. My reflex to splice a detail onto a notion with `;` tends to *fragment* rather than add information — and here it literally caused my misread. The fix is plain sentence breaks (or, if the detail genuinely subordinates, restructure rather than semicolon-splice).

Two follow-ups for you:

- This is an **uncommitted working-copy change** in `cli.rs` — I haven't committed it (per the commit-only-when-asked rule). It's a small standalone polish, not part of the `0.3.0` ladder, so let me know how you want it to land — its own tiny fix commit, or folded into something else.
- AGENTS.md already has a "Semicolons inside bullets" rule, but your point is broader: semicolons in *running prose* that split a notion without adding info. I can tighten that section to cover the running-prose case if you'd like a durable record of the preference — but I won't touch AGENTS.md unless you want me to (your call, since it's project doc).

content.text: Good distinction — the remedy isn't the same in both places. In a bullet you demote the semicolon-joined clause to an indented sub-bullet; in running prose there's no sub-bullet to demote into, so the fix is a plain sentence break (or a restructure). I'll tighten the rule to cover both cases and name which remedy applies where, and broaden the heading from "inside bullets" since it now spans both (nothing links to the old anchor).

input.old_string:
### Semicolons inside bullets

A bullet that joins multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`)
is a list hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running prose
(intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not absolute: very
short clauses or tight pairs can stay joined inside a bullet when
breaking would be more noise than signal.
input.new_string:
### Semicolons

A semicolon that splices a detail onto a notion without adding
information just fragments the sentence, and the split reads as a
boundary that invites a misread. Two surfaces, two remedies:

- **Inside a bullet** — a bullet joining multiple clauses with
  semicolons (`A; B; C`) is a list hiding inside running prose.
  Break the clauses into indented sub-bullets so the structure
  shows.
- **In running prose** (intro paragraphs, doc comments, help text,
  commit bodies) there is no sub-bullet to demote into. Use a
  plain sentence break — a period — instead, or restructure when
  the detail genuinely subordinates.

Not absolute: a true sentence-join of two closely related
independent clauses, or a very short tight pair inside a bullet,
can stay joined when breaking would be more noise than signal.
