content.text: "Commit" defaults to `vc-x1 push` (land + publish) per AGENTS.md, and pushes need per-push approval plus the per-commit flow — so let me read the cycle protocol before doing anything, as AGENTS.md requires.

content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
4	contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.
5	
6	The artifact a cycle produces is whatever the bot generates from
7	the conversation — code, prose, an image, a song, a screenplay.
8	The steps below use a Rust crate as the running example (the
9	cargo cycle, `Cargo.toml` versioning); substitute your medium's
10	equivalents — this project's manifest is recorded in
11	[versioning.md](versioning.md).
12	
13	## Cycles
14	
15	A cycle has three phases:
16	
17	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
18	  first commit, when it needs setup (a lightweight cycle omits
19	  it and starts at `-1` — see
20	  [versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)). Sets up the
21	  cycle:
22	  - Bump the version-of-record to `X.Y.Z-0` (where it lives
23	    and the suffix scheme are project-specific — see
24	    [versioning.md](versioning.md)).
25	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
26	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
27	    statement + plan ladder).
28	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
29	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
30	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
31	  needs; each runs through the
32	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
33	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
34	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
35	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
36	    `## Done`.
37	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
38	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
39	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
40	    changed.
41	
42	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
43	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
44	result must always be published at close-out. See
45	[Pushing](#pushing).
46	
47	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
48	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
49	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
50	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
51	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
52	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
53	for the local-ladder mechanics.
54	
55	## Chores sections
56	
57	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
58	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
59	general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
60	should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
61	chores file.
62	
63	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
64	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
65	with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
66	followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. The narrative
67	body stays empty until close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out));
68	the `Commits:` line is backfilled later, once the commit is
69	permanent (see [Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).
70	
71	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
72	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
73	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
74	
75	### Commits backfill
76	
77	A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
78	records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
79	lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
80	release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
81	squash rewrites it on the way. So:
82	
83	- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
84	- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
85	  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
86	  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
87	  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
88	  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
89	  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
90	  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
91	  invalidate.
92	
93	Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
94	section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
95	with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
96	(format in AGENTS.md
97	[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
98	A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
99	deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
100	`git log --grep "<title>"`.
101	
102	The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
103	**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
104	close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
105	SHA must be permanent.
106	
107	## Preparation
108	
109	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
110	setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
111	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):
112	
113	- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
114	  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
115	  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
116	  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
117	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
118	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
119	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
120	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
121	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
122	  - A **plan ladder**.
123	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
124	  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
125	  cycle's anticipated close-out title.
126	
127	## Work-N
128	
129	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
130	implement the change. As many as needed:
131	
132	- Each commit runs through the
133	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
134	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
135	  visibility).
136	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
137	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
138	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
139	  scope grows enough (see
140	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
141	
142	## Close-out
143	
144	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
145	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
146	what happens post-squash:
147	
148	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
149	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
150	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
151	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
152	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
153	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
154	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
155	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
156	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
157	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
158	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
159	    back-reference.
160	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
161	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
162	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
163	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
164	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
165	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
166	
167	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
168	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
169	see [Pushing](#pushing).
170	
171	## Numbering
172	
173	Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
174	Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
175	recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
176	nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
177	format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
178	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
179	single source of truth for this repo's versioning.
180	
181	## Per-commit flow
182	
183	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
184	through:
185	
186	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
187	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
188	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
189	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
190	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
191	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
192	4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
193	   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
194	   example the cargo cycle is:
195	   1. `cargo fmt`
196	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
197	   3. `cargo test`
198	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
199	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
200	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
201	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
202	   changes and we iterate until complete.
203	6. **Write the commit description** — see
204	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
205	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
206	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
207	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
208	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
209	   keeps the verb visible):
210	
211	   ```
212	   jj commit -m \
213	   "<type>: <short description>" \
214	   -m "<intro paragraph>
215	
216	   - file1: gist
217	   - file2: gist
218	
219	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
220	   -R .
221	   ```
222	
223	**Two overrides apply:**
224	
225	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
226	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
227	  surface it; don't push through.
228	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
229	  a review or question forward.
230	
231	## Commit description
232	
233	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):
234	
235	```
236	<type>: <short description>
237	```
238	
239	Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
240	version-of-record — where it lives and its bump cadence, see
241	[versioning.md](versioning.md) — is useful for confirming
242	you're running the version you're testing, not a per-commit
243	title marker. We think per-commit version-in-title is
244	unstable on large projects: many changes are in flight
245	simultaneously, and a
246	version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
247	titles don't carry one.
248	
249	### Title
250	
251	- ≤50 chars total.
252	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
253	  `docs`, `chore`.
254	- Favor terse phrasings.
255	
256	### Body
257	
258	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
259	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
260	
261	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
262	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
263	  files with multiple distinct changes:
264	
265	  ```
266	  - path/to/file1
267	    - first distinct change
268	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
269	       with continuation indented 5
270	  ```
271	
272	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
273	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
274	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
275	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
276	
277	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
278	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
279	
280	### Trailer
281	
282	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
283	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
284	in AGENTS.md for the convention.
285	
286	For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
287	trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
288	space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
289	hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.
290	
291	## Reviewing changes
292	
293	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
294	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
295	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
296	
297	- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
298	- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
299	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
300	- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev
301	
302	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
303	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
304	`jj show -r @-`.
305	
306	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
307	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
308	are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).
309	
310	## Pushing
311	
312	### Policy
313	
314	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
315	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
316	the cycle's result must be published.
317	
318	**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
319	cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
320	happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
321	published and explicitly approved that specific push.
322	Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
323	the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.
324	
325	**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
326	the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
327	commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
328	hard stop after push/finalize — are the *interactive default*.
329	They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
330	bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
331	and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
332	through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
333	continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:
334	
335	- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
336	  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
337	  completion.
338	- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
339	  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
340	- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
341	  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
342	  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
343	  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
344	  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
345	  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
346	  reaches this.
347	- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
348	  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
349	- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
350	  per-push approval.
351	
352	### Shape at close-out push
353	
354	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
355	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
356	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
357	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
358	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
359	
360	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
361	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
362	  focused on one or two files.
363	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
364	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
365	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
366	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
367	  for the full setup sequence.
368	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
369	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
370	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
371	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
372	
373	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
374	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
375	
376	### Merge non-ff recipe
377	
378	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
379	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
380	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
381	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
382	Work commit:
383	
384	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
385	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
386	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
387	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
388	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
389	
390	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
391	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
392	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
393	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
394	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
395	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
396	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
397	
398	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
399	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
400	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
401	push force-updates `main`.
402	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
403	the close-out push.
404	
405	### vc-x1 push wrapper
406	
407	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
408	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
409	
410	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
411	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
412	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
413	planned:
414	
415	- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
416	- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
417	- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).
418	
419	### .claude cadence
420	
421	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
422	with every code commit in that push.
423	
424	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
425	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
426	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
427	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
428	
429	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
430	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
431	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
432	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
433	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
434	
435	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
436	session information created, which we don't worry about.
437	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
438	remain empty.
439	
440	### Bot communication at the reviews
441	
442	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
443	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
444	
445	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
446	  "Work complete. Please review."
447	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
448	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
449	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
450	  --body ...` invocation by default.
451	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
452	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
453	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
454	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
455	
456	### After push or finalize: stop and wait
457	
458	After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
459	**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
460	.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
461	hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
462	proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
463	user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
464	step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
465	stop for the whole turn.
466	
467	`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
468	then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
469	no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
470	closing words *before* invoking it. The harness rejects an empty
471	turn, so it may force a visible token after the tool returns; if
472	so, emit a bare acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a
473	summary, verification, or next-step offer.
474	
475	Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
476	squashes → pushes):
477	
478	- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
479	  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
480	  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
481	  the invoke — into the snapshot.
482	- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
483	  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
484	  the bot to do more work.
485	- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
486	  after the tool returns. The harness may force a visible token
487	  (it rejects an empty turn) — keep it to a bare acknowledgment,
488	  never a summary or a next step.
489	
490	**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
491	finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
492	saying, say it *before* the invoke.
493	
494	### Recovery
495	
496	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
497	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
498	  finalize by hand:
499	
500	  ```
501	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
502	  ```
503	
504	- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
505	  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
506	  extra activity by the bot's agents).
507	  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
508	    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
509	    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
510	    *after* the snapshot.
511	  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
512	    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
513	    change; it is not under the user's control.)
514	  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
515	    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
516	    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
517	  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
518	    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
519	    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
520	    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
521	    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
522	    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
523	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
524	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
525	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
526	  stale stage.
527	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
528	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
529	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
530	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
531	  push.
532	
533	## Iterative work
534	
535	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
536	from incremental review, loop:
537	
538	1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
539	2. Make the next round of changes.
540	3. User reviews the round (see
541	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
542	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
543	   new empty `@`.
544	5. If not done, go to step 2.
545	
546	Same jj mechanics as a
547	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
548	single-commit scope — the version
549	doesn't change.
550	
551	## Sub-cycle ladders
552	
553	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
554	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
555	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
556	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
557	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
558	for review and bisection only.
559	
560	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
561	
562	For each Work commit in the ladder:
563	
564	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
565	2. Do the commit's work.
566	3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
567	   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
568	   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
569	   suite, raising bisection cost.
570	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
571	   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
572	   into one final commit.
573	
574	### Navigating the ladder
575	
576	Common moves:
577	
578	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
579	  from its base.
580	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
581	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
582	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
583	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
584	  isolation.
585	
586	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
587	descendants auto-rebase.
588	
589	### Close-out: squash the ladder
590	
591	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
592	
593	```
594	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
595	```
596	
597	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
598	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
599	After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
600	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
601	
602	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
603	
604	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
605	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
606	
607	### Recovery
608	
609	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
610	prior commits:
611	
612	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
613	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
614	  parent.
615	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
616	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
617	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
618	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
619	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
620	  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
621	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
622	
623	# References
624	
625	- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
626	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
627	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
628	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
629	  for squash-recipe experiments.
630	- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
631	  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
632	  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
633	  bisection cost.
634

file.content:
# Cycle protocol

This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.

The artifact a cycle produces is whatever the bot generates from
the conversation — code, prose, an image, a song, a screenplay.
The steps below use a Rust crate as the running example (the
cargo cycle, `Cargo.toml` versioning); substitute your medium's
equivalents — this project's manifest is recorded in
[versioning.md](versioning.md).

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
  first commit, when it needs setup (a lightweight cycle omits
  it and starts at `-1` — see
  [versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)). Sets up the
  cycle:
  - Bump the version-of-record to `X.Y.Z-0` (where it lives
    and the suffix scheme are project-specific — see
    [versioning.md](versioning.md)).
  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
    statement + plan ladder).
  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
  needs; each runs through the
  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
    `## Done`.
  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
    [chores section](#chores-sections).
  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
    changed.

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
chores file.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. The narrative
body stays empty until close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out));
the `Commits:` line is backfilled later, once the commit is
permanent (see [Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).

Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).

### Commits backfill

A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
squash rewrites it on the way. So:

- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
  invalidate.

Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
(format in AGENTS.md
[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
`git log --grep "<title>"`.

The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
SHA must be permanent.

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):

- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
  - A **plan ladder**.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
  cycle's anticipated close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
what happens post-squash:

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
  placeholder opened during Preparation):
  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
    chores `##` header already carries that string).
  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
    back-reference.
  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
  - Replace `## In Progress` with
    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
single source of truth for this repo's versioning.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
   example the cargo cycle is:
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description>" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):

```
<type>: <short description>
```

Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
version-of-record — where it lives and its bump cadence, see
[versioning.md](versioning.md) — is useful for confirming
you're running the version you're testing, not a per-commit
title marker. We think per-commit version-in-title is
unstable on large projects: many changes are in flight
simultaneously, and a
version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
titles don't carry one.

### Title

- ≤50 chars total.
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
in AGENTS.md for the convention.

For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:

- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
published and explicitly approved that specific push.
Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.

**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
hard stop after push/finalize — are the *interactive default*.
They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:

- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
  completion.
- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
  reaches this.
- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
  per-push approval.

### Shape at close-out push

At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
needs approval), so choose deliberately.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
session information created, which we don't worry about.
The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
remain empty.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it. The harness rejects an empty
turn, so it may force a visible token after the tool returns; if
so, emit a bare acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a
summary, verification, or next-step offer.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
squashes → pushes):

- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
  the invoke — into the snapshot.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
  the bot to do more work.
- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
  after the tool returns. The harness may force a visible token
  (it rejects an empty turn) — keep it to a bare acknowledgment,
  never a summary or a next step.

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
  extra activity by the bot's agents).
  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
    *after* the snapshot.
  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
    change; it is not under the user's control.)
  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

## Iterative work

When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
from incremental review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. User reviews the round (see
   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
   new empty `@`.
5. If not done, go to step 2.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope — the version
doesn't change.

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
   suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
   into one final commit.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

# References

- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
  bisection cost.

file.content:
# Versioning

How this project versions its commits and the running
artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
this file is copied **verbatim**, with
[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
edits.

## Terms

Three names, used as defined here across
[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:

- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
  version, in the project's manifest (see
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
  a built or running artifact derives from it.
- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.

## Recording the version-of-record

Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
your medium:

- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
    committed config it's sourced from)
  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
    needed
- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
    store it verbatim
  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
    version, just the stored spelling
- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
  version-of-record:
  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
  this project follows the per-commit preference.

## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)

Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
the exact commit it came from.

- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
  if it differs.
- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
  contain (see the cycle protocol's
  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).

## Step numbering

The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
renumbering siblings.

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
every commit carries a distinct version.

message.content: How about "The output-config verbs each own one register. Adding `--persist` on a `set-*` causes the register to be written to flash." The "A bare `set-bin` .. " is stated in the `set-bin` below and it is unrelated to the intro paragraph. But actually what does "re-enable the current mask" actually mean?

content.text: Good catches on both. Let me confirm the `set-bin` parse so I describe "re-enable the current mask" accurately, and apply your reword.

Confirmed: bare `set-bin` (no `=`) → `BinSet::Enable`, which in lib.rs:226 does `(on_port, r.mask)` — keeps the existing mask, just sets the async port.

**What "re-enable the current mask" means:** register 75 holds a *Common-group field bitmask* (the `r.mask` — which fields stream: time, accel, gyro, …). "Re-enable the current mask" = a bare `set-bin` (no `=fields`) turns binary streaming back **on using whatever field set is already stored in reg 75**, without redefining the fields. The mask persists in the register even when streaming is off (`set-bin=off` sets the port/mode to 0 but leaves `r.mask` intact), so after `set-bin=off` a later bare `set-bin` resumes the *same* fields. It's the "resume what I had" verb. You're right that it's binary-specific and belongs in the `set-bin` row below, not the shared intro.

Applying your reword (and dropping the bare-`set-bin` sentence from the intro):

content.text:
Renders as a clean two-sentence paragraph, and the bare-`set-bin` explanation stays where it belongs — in the `set-bin` row below ("Set the binary field mask, or `off`. Bare `set-bin` re-enables the current mask.").

Updated work for review:

- **`src/cli.rs`** — intro paragraph is now: *"The output-config verbs each own one register. Adding `--persist` on a `set-*` causes the register to be written to flash."* (dropped the binary-specific bare-`set-bin` sentence; it's already documented in the `set-bin` row).
- **`AGENTS.md`** — `### Semicolons` rule (unchanged from before).
- **`Cargo.toml` / `Cargo.lock`** — `0.3.0-5.1`.

Is the work complete, or do you also want to tighten the `set-bin` row's "re-enables the current mask" wording (now that we've pinned down what it means — e.g. "resume streaming the configured fields")? Once you confirm, I'll re-run the cargo cycle and show the commit title + body for approval.

lastPrompt: How about "The output-config verbs each own one register. Adding `--persist` on a `set-*` causes the register to be written to flash." The "A bare `set-bin` .. " is stated in the `set-bin` below and i…

message.content: Also tighten the set-bin read something like; "Updates the fields in bin register from the value in flash".

content.text:
Hold on — I want to flag that the suggested wording would be inaccurate, before I write it. A bare `set-bin` doesn't read flash, and it doesn't update the fields:

- It sends `VNRRG,75` to read the **current register** (the volatile working copy — lib.rs:213), keeps the existing field mask `r.mask` unchanged, and sets the output port to turn streaming back **on** (lib.rs:226 `BinSet::Enable => (on_port, r.mask)`).
- Flash never enters this path. Flash is touched only by `--persist` (a `VNWNV` save), and the register is *loaded* from flash only at power-on/reset. A runtime bare `set-bin` reads the live register, not flash.

So "Updates the fields ... from the value in flash" is wrong twice over: it reads the volatile register (not flash), and it doesn't change the fields (it re-enables output of the fields already set). If the register currently has, say, time+accel and you `set-bin=off` then bare `set-bin`, you get time+accel streaming again — same fields, just switched back on.

Accurate alternatives for that line — pick one (or steer me):

1. *"Bare `set-bin` resumes output of the fields already configured."*
2. *"Bare `set-bin` re-enables streaming without changing the fields (e.g. after `set-bin=off`)."*
3. Keep "re-enables the current mask" but gloss *mask* → *"re-enables the current field set."*

Which do you want? (Or if you actually intended a *new* behavior — read the field config back from flash into the register — that's a feature change, not a doc tweak, and a different conversation.)

message.content: Glad we touched this because my interpretation was wrong. You have to explain more preciely what is being "re-enabled" and where is it coming from if I do a `set-bin=time,ypr` and then a bare `set-bin` what is the result?

content.text:
Good — let me trace your exact scenario through the code precisely. The key fact: **"the current mask" lives in the device's volatile register 75, not in the tool and not (at runtime) in flash.** Every `set-bin*` command starts by reading it back with `VNRRG,75` (lib.rs:213).

**Step 1 — `set-bin=time,ypr`:**
- `value="time,ypr"` → `BinSet::Fields` (cli.rs:382). `parse_fields` orders by bit → time (bit 0), ypr (bit 3), so the mask is `(1<<0)|(1<<3)` = `0x0009` (lib.rs:228).
- It reads the current register, then writes `VNWRG,75,<port>,<divisor>,01,0009` — enabling output on the port (port 2 if binary was off, else the current port) at the existing rate divisor (lib.rs:223,234,238).
- Net: register 75 now holds **mask `0x0009` (time+ypr), streaming on, at the prior rate**. Volatile — no flash write without `--persist`.

**Step 2 — bare `set-bin`:**
- `value=None` → `BinSet::Enable` (cli.rs:380).
- It reads register 75 back: that read returns `r.mask = 0x0009`, `r.async_mode = 2` (it's currently streaming), `r.divisor` = the current rate.
- `BinSet::Enable => (on_port, r.mask)` (lib.rs:226): it keeps `r.mask` unchanged and sets the port. Since streaming is already on, `on_port = r.async_mode` (lib.rs:223), so it writes `VNWRG,75,2,<divisor>,01,0009` — **byte-for-byte the same register it just read.**

**Result of your scenario: nothing changes.** Binary keeps streaming time+ypr at the same rate. The bare `set-bin` is an idempotent rewrite here, because streaming was already on.

The bare `set-bin` only *does* something when binary is currently **off**:
- `set-bin=time,ypr` → streaming time+ypr.
- `set-bin=off` → `BinSet::Off => (0, r.mask)` (lib.rs:225): sets mode 0 (streaming off) but **leaves the mask `0x0009` sitting in the register**.
- bare `set-bin` → reads mask `0x0009` (still there), `on_port = 2` (because async_mode is 0 → lib.rs:223), writes it back with output enabled → **resumes streaming time+ypr.**

So "re-enable the current mask" precisely means: *re-read whatever field set is still sitting in register 75 and turn its output back on.* It's the "resume after `set-bin=off`" verb. Two sharp edges worth knowing:

- **It never reads flash.** The mask source is the live volatile register — which is the last `set-bin=…` value, or (only at power-on/reset) the flash default.
- **Re-enabling always targets port 2** (the RPi5 TTL header), regardless of which port it was on before the `off`, because `on_port` defaults to 2 whenever `async_mode == 0` (lib.rs:223). If you'd been streaming on port 1, `off`, then bare `set-bin`, it comes back on **port 2**, not port 1.

Given that, the most accurate one-liner for the `set-bin` row is something like:

> *"Bare `set-bin` resumes binary output of the fields still in the register (use after `set-bin=off`); it re-enables on port 2."*

Want me to use that (or a trimmed version), and should I also note the port-2 detail in the help, or leave that as code-level nuance?

message.content: I suggest we add `set-bin=on` and a `set-bin=time,ypr` just sets the fields doesn't change on or off. (we should probably do `set|=time,ypr` which or's in the new fields and `set^=time,ypr` toggles just kidding but it makes sense to a programmer). So if it's on or off `set-bin=time,ypr` just redefines the fields. It it's off a `set-bin=time,ypr set-ascii=off set-bin=on` would do what you expect in that order. Thoughts?

content.text:
Love it — this is the right instinct, and it's a real improvement, not a doc tweak. The core idea is **orthogonality**: make "what fields" and "is it streaming" independent knobs instead of conflating them. Today `set-bin=time,ypr` secretly also flips streaming on, which is exactly the hidden side effect that made "re-enables the current mask" confusing in the first place. Thoughts, in order:

**The model is clean and already half-built.** You'd end up with three orthogonal knobs on register 75:
- `set-bin=<fields>` — redefine the field mask, leave on/off untouched
- `set-bin=on` / `set-bin=off` — toggle streaming, leave the mask untouched
- `set-bin-hz=<hz>` — set the rate (already orthogonal — it just writes the divisor)

That's consistent: all three "set" one thing and only that thing. `set-bin-hz` already behaves this way, so this brings `set-bin` into line with its sibling.

**It composes perfectly with the `0.3.0-6` step grammar.** Your example `set-bin=time,ypr set-ascii=off set-bin=on` *is* a single-connection multi-step line — which is exactly what `-6` builds (shell-word steps, one port open, left-to-right). The orthogonal verbs are what make that line read declaratively. So I'd fold this into `-6` (or a small dedicated step right before it) rather than treat it as separate.

**Edge cases to pin down before coding:**
- **Enable target port.** When `set-bin=on` flips from off, the register's `async_mode` is 0, so we've *lost* which port it was on — today's code defaults to port 2 (RPi5 TTL, lib.rs:223). With orthogonal on/off that default becomes more visible: `on port1 → off → on` silently lands on port 2. Either keep port-2-default (document it) or store/restore the last port. Port 2 is right for this rig, so I'd document it and move on.
- **`set-bin=on` with an empty mask** (never configured, or mask 0) → the device rejects a zero-field frame with `$VNERR`. Guard it: "no fields configured — `set-bin=<fields>` first."
- **Bare `set-bin`** — make it an alias for `set-bin=on` (least surprise) or drop it with a hint. I'd alias it.
- **Backward compat** — the README/`--help` examples (`set-bin=time,accel,gyro` then `set-bin-hz=200`) currently rely on the implicit enable; they'd need a trailing `set-bin=on`. Fine since we're mid-redesign of the whole grammar, but it's a docs sweep.

**Symmetry with `set-ascii`.** For consistency the same split wants to apply to ASCII: `set-ascii=<preset>` redefines, `set-ascii=on`/`off` toggles. Right now `set-ascii=off` exists but a preset implicitly enables — same conflation. Worth deciding now whether to do both verbs together (consistent, slightly bigger) or just `set-bin` first. I'd do both so the grammar is uniform.

**`|=` / `^=`:** the toggle is a joke, agreed, but `set-bin+=time` (OR-in a field) is genuinely sensible and cheap to parse later. I'd note it as a future nicety and keep the first cut minimal — `=`, `on`, `off` only. YAGNI until someone wants incremental field edits.

**Implication for the in-flight docs commit (`0.3.0-5.1`):** good news — it's untouched by this. We only edited the *intro* paragraph (generic, survives the redesign) and the `AGENTS.md` semicolon rule. We never edited the `set-bin` row's "re-enables the current mask" line — and now we *shouldn't*, because this redesign will rewrite it. So the wording question that kicked this off resolves cleanly: don't tighten that line, change the behavior instead. The docs commit stays as-is and lands independently.

So my recommendation: **finalize the `0.3.0-5.1` docs commit as it stands now**, and I'll capture "decompose `set-bin`/`set-ascii` into orthogonal fields/on/off verbs" as a `## Todo` (or a `-6` design note in chores-01), to land with the step grammar. Want me to proceed to the commit description for `0.3.0-5.1`, and draft the Todo entry for the orthogonal-verbs redesign?

101	## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §3.2.8–3.2.10 (registers), §2 (message format); vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
102	Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
103	per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
104	`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.
105	
106	### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
107	Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:
108	
109	| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
110	|---|---|---|---|---|
111	| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
112	| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
113	| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
114	| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
115	| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
116	| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
117	| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
118	| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |
119	| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |
120	| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |
121	
122	Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).
123	Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.
124	Code: `FIELDS` (`bench --bin --fields`).
125	
126	## Error responses — ICD §1.5 (Table 1.6, $VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`
127	`$VNERR,<code>*XX` — `<code>` is **hex**.
128	
129	| code | meaning | | code | meaning |
130	|---|---|---|---|---|
131	| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
132	| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
133	| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
134	| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
135	| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
136	| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
137	| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |
138	
139	`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
140	`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §3.2.8–3.2.10 (registers), §2 (message format); vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |
| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |
| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |

Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).
Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.
Code: `FIELDS` (`bench --bin --fields`).

## Error responses — ICD §1.5 (Table 1.6, $VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`
`$VNERR,<code>*XX` — `<code>` is **hex**.

| code | meaning | | code | meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |

`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

content.text:
Confirmed against `REFERENCE.md` — so here's the precise answer on **other registers for this syntax:**

- **Registers 76 & 77 (Binary Output 2/3)** — structurally *identical* to 75 (`REFERENCE.md:101`): same `asyncMode` / `rateDivisor` / group-mask layout, just two more independent outputs. The entire `set-bin` grammar (`=fields`, `=on/off`, `+=`/`-=`, `set-bin-hz`) generalizes to them verbatim — the tool would only need a way to target output 2/3.
- **Reg 75's own `asyncMode`** is itself a **serial-port bitmask** (`REFERENCE.md:102`) — which ports the output streams on. That's another set-valued field, so `+=`/`-=` (add/remove a port) or per-port on/off is the same syntax. This is exactly the "enable defaults to port 2" nuance from before.
- **Other output groups within 75** (Common is just group `0x01`; Time/IMU/Attitude/… each have their own mask) — if the tool ever grows past Common-only, each group's mask is another `+=`/`-=` target.
- **Not `set-ascii`** — reg 6 is a single-valued *preset* enum (off/ypr/qtn/…), so `=on`/`=off` fits but `+=`/`-=` does not (you can't OR presets). The set-arithmetic is specifically for the bitmask registers.

Now drafting the two todos:

input.old_string:
`test-data/both-streams.bin`. [[8]]

## Done
input.new_string:
`test-data/both-streams.bin`. [[8]]

2. Decompose `set-bin` (and `set-ascii`) into orthogonal verbs:
   a field/preset write that never toggles streaming, plus
   explicit `=on` / `=off`. Today `set-bin=<fields>` secretly
   enables output too, conflating "what fields" with "whether it
   streams". Lands with the step grammar so a single line —
   `set-bin=time,ypr set-ascii=off set-bin=on` — reads
   declaratively. Pin down on the way: `=on` defaults to port 2,
   an empty-mask guard before `=on`, and bare `set-bin` as an
   `=on` alias. [[1]]

3. `set-bin+=<fields>` / `set-bin-=<fields>`: OR-in / mask-out
   Common fields incrementally instead of restating the whole
   set. The set-arithmetic generalizes to the other bitmask
   registers — Binary Output 2/3 (regs 76/77, identical layout)
   and reg 75's `asyncMode` serial-port mask — but not to
   `set-ascii` (reg 6 is a single-valued preset, so only
   `=on`/`=off` fits there). Builds on the orthogonal `set-bin`
   verbs. [[1]]

## Done

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 feat: decompose output config into register verbs —
     `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `set-ascii-hz` (reg 7),
     `get/set-bin` (reg 75), and `set-bin-hz` (the reg-75
     divisor), all as `key=value` tokens. Drops `get-hz`/`set-hz`.
     Keeps the legacy configure/measure `bench` as a parallel path
     until -5. (done)
   - 0.3.0-5 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. Now exercisable via the -4 verbs (`set-bin`
     then `bench`). (done — impl landed; an intermittent
     cold-open 0/0 remains, tracked as Todo #1)
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     merge `set-bin`+`set-bin-hz` in one word into a single reg-75
     write.
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Fix passive `bench` intermittent zero-parse: it sometimes
   reports `ASCII: none / Binary: none` then works on a re-run.
   The symptom varies — full ~269 kbit/s in some failures, a low
   ~24 kbit/s in another — so start by checking device state
   (`get-bin` / `get-ascii` / `rrg 5`), then capture a real
   failure via an env-gated raw dump and diff against the clean
   `test-data/both-streams.bin`. [[8]]

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: default RPi5 UART, fix binary port on TTL [[2]],[[3]]
- fix: bench silences async before binary config [[4]]

- feat: passive bench measures the live stream [[7]]

**Drafted todos** (added to `notes/todo.md` as ranks #2 and #3, below the zero-parse bug #1 — easily reorderable):

> **2.** Decompose `set-bin` (and `set-ascii`) into orthogonal verbs: a field/preset write that never toggles streaming, plus explicit `=on`/`=off`. Today `set-bin=<fields>` secretly enables output too, conflating "what fields" with "whether it streams". Lands with the step grammar so `set-bin=time,ypr set-ascii=off set-bin=on` reads declaratively. Pin down: `=on` defaults to port 2, an empty-mask guard before `=on`, and bare `set-bin` as an `=on` alias. [[1]]
>
> **3.** `set-bin+=<fields>` / `set-bin-=<fields>`: OR-in / mask-out Common fields incrementally. Generalizes to the other bitmask registers — Binary Output 2/3 (regs 76/77) and reg 75's `asyncMode` port mask — but not `set-ascii` (reg 6 is a single-valued preset). Builds on the orthogonal `set-bin` verbs. [[1]]

**Proposed commit** (I folded the todos into the one `docs:` commit — all notes/doc changes; say the word if you'd rather they land separately):
