content.text: Let me confirm the exact push invocation before I run it (we've done the diff review in conversation, so I'll skip the wrapper's interactive gate and pass the message directly).

content.content:
vc-x1 0.65.2-0

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate

file.content:
## Register 6 — Async Data Output Type / ADOR — ICD §3.2.3 (Table 3.6); vnsdk `AsyncOutputType::Ador`
A **single** selection (one preset *or* off), not a bitmask. **Default = 14 (YMR).**

| ADOR | value | message (source register) |
|---|---|---|
| OFF | 0 | async off |
| YPR | 1 | Yaw,Pitch,Roll (reg 8) |
| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |
| MAG | 10 | Magnetic (reg 17) |
| ACC | 11 | Acceleration (reg 18) |
| GYR | 12 | Angular Rate (reg 19) |
| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |
| YMR | 14 | YPR,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 27) — **default** |
| YBA | 16 | YPR,Body Accel,Rates (reg 239) |
| YIA | 17 | YPR,Inertial Accel,Rates (reg 240) |
| IMU | 19 | IMU Measurements (reg 54) |
| DTV | 30 | Delta Theta & Delta Velocity (reg 80) |
| HVE | 34 | Heave (reg 115) |

Code: `ASCII_TYPES` (`bench --type`). The SDK also defines GPS/INS values
(GPS/GPE/INS/INE/ISL/ISE/G2S/G2E) — **not applicable to the VN-100** (no GNSS).

## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD §3.2.4 (Table 3.9); vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
`0(off), 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200` Hz. **Max 200.** ✓ (40 default)
Code: `VALID_RATES` (non-zero values; `set-hz` / ASCII `bench`).

content.text:
Pulled the preset table (`REFERENCE.md:74`, `cli.rs:22`). ASCII presets are whole canned messages, so "fewer fields" = a leaner preset. Rough on-wire sizes (sign+3int+dot+3frac ≈ 9 B/float, plus `$VNxxx,…*XX\r\n` framing), and what each costs at 40 Hz on the 11,520 B/s link:

| preset | floats | ~bytes/msg | @40 Hz | % link |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ymr` (current) | 12 | ~121 | 4,840 B/s | 42% |
| `mar` / `yba` / `yia` | 9 | ~93 | 3,720 B/s | 32% |
| `qtn` | 4 | ~47 | 1,880 B/s | 16% |
| **`ypr`** | **3** | **~38** | **1,520 B/s** | **13%** |

`ypr` (yaw/pitch/roll only) is the natural minimal attitude preset. Dropping `ymr`→`ypr` frees ~3,300 B/s, and that's exactly what lets binary run at the **200 Hz** fc.py target alongside ASCII:

- **ASCII `ypr` @40 Hz + binary `time,accel,gyro` @200 Hz**: 1,520 + (38 B × 200) = 1,520 + 7,600 = **9,120 B/s ≈ 79%** of the link. Fits, with margin — clearly not a saturation case.
- Conservative fallback: same but binary **@100 Hz** → 5,320 B/s ≈ **46%**.

Commands (the orthogonality wrinkle is moot on the current tool — `set-ascii=ypr` both selects and streams since reg 6 has no separate enable bit, and `set-bin=<fields>` enables binary on port 2):

```
rw-vn100 set-ascii=ypr --baud 115200
rw-vn100 set-bin=time,accel,gyro --baud 115200
rw-vn100 set-bin-hz=200 --baud 115200
rw-vn100 bench --baud 115200 2
```

One caveat that matters for the **actual cold-open hunt** (not for this first check): these writes are volatile, so a power cycle reverts to `ymr` + binary-off. To reproduce the *cold-open* zero-parse with both streams live at the first open, we'd need to `--persist` the dual config and then power-cycle, so the very first `bench` is the cold open against both streams. For right now, volatile is fine — we just want to confirm both streams coexist at 115200.

Two quick calls before we go:
1. **Binary rate** — start at **200 Hz** (the real target, 79%) or the conservative **100 Hz** (46%)?
2. **Who runs it** — you at the rpi5 console (as you did with the earlier benches), or want me to run them via Bash here? (If you run it, I'd avoid touching the port to prevent contention.)

file.content:
## fix: bench silences async before binary config

Commits: [[5]]

`bench --bin` could reject a binary config with `$VNERR` 0x0C
("insufficient baud rate") at a low baud even when the binary stream
fits the link with room to spare. The cause was write ordering:
bench configured the binary output (reg 75) while ASCII async (reg
7) was still streaming, so the device's fit check saw the *combined*
ASCII + binary load. The fix silences ASCII async before writing the
binary config, so the check sees only the binary load.

### Bench combined-load fit check at low baud

Observed 2026-06-26 on the RPi5 TTL header (`/dev/ttyAMA0`) running
`bench --bin` after an in-session baud switch to 57600:

- At 57600 with ASCII async still at 40 Hz, `VNWRG,75,2,20,01,0101`
  (time+accel, 26 B/frame, 40 Hz) was rejected with `$VNERR` 0x0C.
- The binary stream alone is ~10 kbit/s — 18% of the 57600 link —
  so it fits easily; only the transient ASCII-40 + binary-40
  combination overran the check.
- Lowering ASCII async to 4 Hz first, then re-running the identical
  bench, the same `VNWRG,75` was accepted and streamed clean at
  39.9 Hz. Only the ASCII rate changed, isolating the combined load
  as the cause.

We think the device enforces `message_size × rate ≤ baud` on every
reg-5 / reg-7 / reg-75 write, against the *sum* of the streams
configured on that port. At 115200 the headroom hid the transient;
at 57600 it surfaced. The ordering was always latent and only became
visible at the lower baud.

The fix reorders `bench_binary`:

- Before: read reg 7 → write reg 75 → write reg 7=0. The fit check
  fired on the reg-75 write, before ASCII was silenced.
- After: read reg 7 → write reg 7=0 → write reg 75, and restore reg
  7 if the reg-75 write still fails — so a genuinely-too-big binary
  frame (e.g. the 38 B frame at 200 Hz / 115200, [[2]]) no longer
  leaves ASCII async switched off.
- `bench_ascii` is unaffected: it has a single stream and already
  writes the message type (reg 6) before the rate (reg 7).

## Rename rdwr_vn100 to rw-vn100

content.text:
Good catch, and I checked the surface for the mechanism: **there's no single global async toggle** in the tool (or the documented register set — no `VNASY`-style pause). Async is **per-stream**:

- **ASCII** — `set-ascii=off` (reg 6 → 0); re-enable with `set-ascii=ypr` (rate in reg 7 is preserved, so it comes back at 40 Hz).
- **Binary** — `set-bin=off` (reg 75 `asyncMode` → 0, mask retained); re-enable with bare `set-bin` or `set-bin=<fields>`.

The old bench's "silence" was exactly this: write reg 7 → 0 before touching reg 75. And the chores section behind that fix confirms *why it matters beyond contention*: **the device runs its `size × rate ≤ baud` fit-check against the *combined* load of all streams on the port, on every reg-5/7/75 write** (chores-01:499). So writing the binary config while ASCII streams makes the device evaluate both together. At 115200 there's headroom, but quieting first is the clean, correct order regardless.

So for change 2, the minimal-contention sequence (I'd run these three; the reg-75 write lands on a quiet wire):

```
set-ascii=off                  # silence ASCII
set-bin=time,accel,gyro        # write reg 75 quiet; binary starts at 40 Hz
set-ascii=ypr                  # re-enable ASCII
```

Then you `bench 2` → expect `ypr` @40 Hz + binary @40 Hz.

**One honest limitation, and it's literally Todo #2:** the current `set-bin=<fields>` *also enables* (no way to define the mask while keeping binary off), so the binary turns on the instant I write it — meaning during that final `set-ascii=ypr` write, binary is already streaming. We can't get a fully-silent "configure everything, then flip on" with today's verbs. The orthogonal `set-bin=on/off` redesign is exactly what would let us stage all the writes quiet and enable last. For now, this three-step is as quiet as it gets.

Want me to run those three, then you bench? Or would you rather I also `set-bin=off` between steps to keep each individual write quiet — at the cost of more reopens?

message.content: Seems we should add set-bin=off/on to make this test work?

content.text:
Agreed — and it's the right call for a sharper reason than convenience. We're about to hunt an *intermittent* bug, and the cardinal rule there is to keep the test harness deterministic so the tool's own behavior isn't a confound. Right now `set-bin=<fields>` enables on the same write that sets the mask, so the finicky reg-75 write can never happen on a fully quiet wire. Decoupling that removes config-time contention as a variable before we start measuring. It's also already-planned work (Todo #2) — we'd just be pulling the binary half forward because the test needs it.

**The actual change** (binary only — ASCII already toggles cleanly via `set-ascii=off` / `set-ascii=ypr`, so it doesn't need this):

- `set-bin=<fields>` — set the mask, **preserve** current on/off (`asyncMode`) instead of force-enabling. *(behavior change)*
- `set-bin=on` — enable: set the output port, keep the mask. *(new)*
- `set-bin=off` — unchanged (disable, retain mask).
- bare `set-bin` — alias of `=on`.
- Guard: `set-bin=on` with an empty mask → error *"no fields configured — `set-bin=<fields>` first"* (avoids the device's zero-field `$VNERR`).
- Enable still defaults to port 2 (RPi5 TTL), as today.

That makes the clean test sequence:

```
set-ascii=off              # quiet
set-bin=time,accel,gyro    # mask only — stays OFF, fully quiet reg-75 write
set-bin=on                 # binary on
set-ascii=ypr              # ascii on
```

Only the two enables touch a live wire, and the sensitive field write is silent.

**It's a real `feat:` commit**, not a quick edit — touches `lib.rs` (the `SetBin` match arm), `cli.rs` (parse `on`, update the `set-bin` help row), the README examples (which rely on the old auto-enable), and the `SetBin` tests. So I'd run it as a proper cycle commit: implement → fmt/clippy/test → show you the diff → commit.

Two things to confirm before I touch code:
1. **Version** — I'd land it as `0.3.0-5.2` (keeps the planned `-6` step-grammar work distinct), and trim Todo #2 down to the remaining parts (ASCII symmetry, `+=`/`-=`). Good, or would you rather it *be* the start of `-6`?
2. **Scope** — binary-only now, ASCII orthogonality stays in the Todo? (I think yes — ASCII off/on already works.)

Once you confirm, I'll implement and show you the diff before committing.

content: Goal: reliably run ASCII+binary together at 115200 to hunt the intermittent cold-open bug. We paused testing to add orthogonal set-bin=on/off so config writes happen on a quiet wire. Next: you confirm version (0.3.0-5.2?) and binary-only scope, then I implement. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content:
I suggest, for now, "bare set-bin" is an error.
How will you know if "no fields are configured" will you read the register 75 first?

content.text:
Both good. Two answers:

**Bare `set-bin` → error: yes, and it's the consistent choice.** Bare `set-ascii` is *already* an error in the tool (cli.rs:509 — "reg 6 has no separate enable bit"), so making bare `set-bin` an error too keeps the two verbs symmetric and forces the intent to be explicit (`=on` / `=off` / `=<fields>`). So `BinSet::Enable` becomes reachable only via `set-bin=on`.

**The empty-mask check — yes, we read register 75 first, and we already do.** Every `set-bin*` command is a read-modify-write: it issues `VNRRG,75` up front (lib.rs:213) and parses the reply into `r` (`async_mode`, `divisor`, `mask`). So `set-bin=on` gets the current mask *for free* from that same read — no extra round-trip. The guard is then just:

- `parse_reg75` returns `mask = 0` whenever the Common group bit is clear or the field mask is literally zero (proto.rs ~217) — i.e. "no Common-group fields configured."
- In the `BinSet::Enable` arm, if `r.mask == 0` → error `"no fields configured — set-bin=<fields> first"`, before we write anything.

The device stays the final arbiter — any *other* rejection (e.g. `$VNERR 0x0C` if a future config doesn't fit the baud) still surfaces through the existing write-path error handling. The mask guard is just a friendly pre-check for the common "forgot to set fields" mistake, costing nothing extra since the read already happened. And in our test sequence the read lands on a quiet wire (ASCII silenced, binary off), so it's clean.

Updated plan, then:
- `set-bin=<fields>` — set mask, preserve on/off
- `set-bin=on` — enable (error if `r.mask == 0`)
- `set-bin=off` — disable, retain mask
- bare `set-bin` — **error** (needs `=<fields>`, `=on`, or `=off`)

Same files as before (`lib.rs`, `cli.rs`, README, tests), landing as `0.3.0-5.2` unless you want it as `-6`. Ready for me to implement and show you the diff?

nps-gnc@rpi5-20cd 26-06-28T17:03:45.802Z:~/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rw-vn100 (main+1)
$ which rw-vn100

Note 1/2 about I ran the test, then a few minutes ago I ran a second test, it was ok. Then 15 seconds after that I ran a third test and no data on ASCII or Binary!

content.text:
Holding — I won't install or touch anything. Let me read that sequence back, because I think it's a baud mismatch, not a dropout:

- **17:03:28 run used `--baud 921600`** while the device is still at **115200** (we only set `ascii=ypr` — volatile — and never issued a `baud` command, so it's at the flash-default 115200). The host was listening 8× too fast, so the 115200 bytes are unintelligible → "none seen." The tool even diagnosed it: *"does --baud match its current rate?"* And the "~46 kbit/s = 5%" line confirms the wire was **active** (real bytes arriving, ~15 kbit/s of VNYPR misread as ~46 kbit/s of garbage at the wrong clock) — not silence, not a wedge.

So that third "no data" is fully explained by the `921600` argument, **not** the cold-open zero-parse bug. The bug signature we're hunting is no-data *at the correct 115200 baud* — and your two 115200 runs (16:29:17, 16:29:51) were both clean VNYPR at 39.6 Hz.

Also noted: you backed up the current binary to `rw-vn100.0.3.0-5.1` before I overwrite it — good call for A/B'ing old vs new. Status on my side: the `0.3.0-5.2` orthogonal-`set-bin` work is implemented in the working copy and passed fmt/clippy/test, but I have **not** installed it (your `cp` backup is intact) and have **not** committed it.

Waiting for note 2/2 before doing anything.

lastPrompt: Before that an error occurred with the "older" code: nps-gnc@rpi5-20cd 26-06-28T16:29:17.727Z:~/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rw-vn100 (main+1) $ rw-vn100 bench 2 --baud 115200 rw-vn100 0.3.0-5.1 Opening /dev/tty…

message.content: I changed the baud, but it should have worked, correct?

content.text:
No — it correctly showed no data, and here's the distinction that bit it:

- **`--baud 921600` did not change the device's baud.** That flag only sets the rate *your host* talks/listens at. The help spells it out: *"Baud to talk to the device NOW; must match the device's CURRENT rate."* It's a listening clock, not a command to the device.
- **`bench` is passive — it never writes register 5** (or anything). So nothing told the VN-100 to switch. It stayed at its flash-default **115200** (we only ever set `ascii=ypr`, volatile; no `baud` command was issued).
- So you had a 115200 talker and a 921600 listener — an 8× mismatch. The bytes arrive but are unintelligible → "none seen," with the ~46 kbit/s "throughput" being that misread garbage. Exactly what the tool's *"does --baud match its current rate?"* hint was pointing at.

The verb that actually changes the device baud is the **`baud`** command (it sends `VNWRG,05` and switches the live connection):

```
rw-vn100 baud 921600          # switch the DEVICE to 921600 (add --persist to keep it)
rw-vn100 bench --baud 921600  # now host and device match
```

Two cautions before you go that route, though:
- **Bug #1 (the wedge):** high-baud *fresh-process* reconnects at 921600 can wedge the UART, recoverable only by power cycle. That was on the RS-232 rig; on this TTL header it's untested (we think it may not reproduce on TTL) — but 921600 is precisely the risky baud.
- We deliberately picked **115200 as the reliable path** for this dual-stream / zero-parse work. Jumping to 921600 reintroduces the wedge as a confound. I'd stay at 115200 until the orthogonal `set-bin` test is done, then change device baud deliberately if we want to probe higher.

So: nothing actually failed there — just a host/device baud mismatch. Want to continue with note 2/2, or pick back up on installing/committing `0.3.0-5.2`?

content.text:
Fixed (comment-only; I'll re-run the cycle right before the push). Proposed commit:

```
feat: orthogonal set-bin verbs (fields vs on/off)

Decouple the binary field mask from streaming on/off so config
writes can land on a quiet wire — set the fields with output off,
then enable. Pulled forward from the verb-redesign Todo because the
dual-stream reliability test needs a contention-free setup.

- src/lib.rs: SetBin no longer enables on a field write. Fields
  preserves async_mode, Enable (=on) sets the port. Guard =on
  against an empty mask (would stream a device-rejected zero-field
  frame). The rate-defaulted note now fires on the field/enable
  paths.
- src/cli.rs
  - add `set-bin=on` parsing
  - make bare `set-bin` an error (enable is explicit now, like
    bare set-ascii)
  - help row → `set-bin=<FIELDS|on|off>`, plus configure→rate→on
    examples
  - update the BinSet doc and the parse test
- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version-of-record 0.3.0-5.1 -> 0.3.0-5.2.
```

One open item — **Todo #2's disposition**, which I have *not* touched yet. Implementing this surfaced a wrinkle: the ASCII half of that todo isn't actually achievable. Register 6 (ADOR) is a single value where `0 = off` and `N = preset-N streaming` — there's **no separate enable bit**, so you can't "set the preset while keeping it off" the way binary now allows. Making `set-ascii` orthogonal would require the tool to *remember* the last preset across invocations, which fights its stateless design.

So my read: **Todo #2 is effectively complete** — binary orthogonality landed here, ASCII is N/A by register design. I'd move it to `## Done` with that note, and keep `#3` (`+=`/`-=`) as the live follow-on.

Two questions before I push:
1. Agree to **close Todo #2** (binary done, ASCII N/A), or keep it open for a stateful "remember last preset" approach?
2. Fold that todo edit into **this** commit, or keep this commit code-only and do the todo bookkeeping separately?

2. fold in the todo edit this commit.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Fix passive `bench` intermittent zero-parse: it sometimes
   reports `ASCII: none / Binary: none` then works on a re-run.
   The symptom varies — full ~269 kbit/s in some failures, a low
   ~24 kbit/s in another — so start by checking device state
   (`get-bin` / `get-ascii` / `rrg 5`), then capture a real
   failure via an env-gated raw dump and diff against the clean
   `test-data/both-streams.bin`. [[8]]

2. Decompose `set-bin` (and `set-ascii`) into orthogonal verbs:
   a field/preset write that never toggles streaming, plus
   explicit `=on` / `=off`. Today `set-bin=<fields>` secretly
   enables output too, conflating "what fields" with "whether it
   streams". Lands with the step grammar so a single line —
   `set-bin=time,ypr set-ascii=off set-bin=on` — reads
   declaratively. Pin down on the way: `=on` defaults to port 2,
   an empty-mask guard before `=on`, and bare `set-bin` as an
   `=on` alias. [[1]]

3. `set-bin+=<fields>` / `set-bin-=<fields>`: OR-in / mask-out
   Common fields incrementally instead of restating the whole
   set. The set-arithmetic generalizes to the other bitmask
   registers — Binary Output 2/3 (regs 76/77, identical layout)
   and reg 75's `asyncMode` serial-port mask — but not to
   `set-ascii` (reg 6 is a single-valued preset, so only
   `=on`/`=off` fits there). Builds on the orthogonal `set-bin`
   verbs. [[1]]

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: default RPi5 UART, fix binary port on TTL [[2]],[[3]]
- fix: bench silences async before binary config [[4]]
- feat: decompose output config into register verbs [[6]]
- feat: passive bench measures the live stream [[7]]

# References

[1]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-passive-bench-composable-command-grammar
[2]: chores/chores-01.md#vn-100-register-75-serial-port-numbering-on-ttl
[3]: chores/chores-01.md#fix-binary-output-targets-the-wrong-vn-100-serial-port-on-ttl
[4]: chores/chores-01.md#fix-bench-silences-async-before-binary-config
[5]: chores/chores-01.md#refactor-split-mainrs-into-lib-modules
[6]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-decompose-output-config-into-register-verbs
[7]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-passive-bench-measures-the-live-stream

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 feat: decompose output config into register verbs —
     `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `set-ascii-hz` (reg 7),
     `get/set-bin` (reg 75), and `set-bin-hz` (the reg-75
     divisor), all as `key=value` tokens. Drops `get-hz`/`set-hz`.
     Keeps the legacy configure/measure `bench` as a parallel path
     until -5. (done)
   - 0.3.0-5 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. Now exercisable via the -4 verbs (`set-bin`
     then `bench`). (done — impl landed; an intermittent
     cold-open 0/0 remains, tracked as Todo #1)
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     merge `set-bin`+`set-bin-hz` in one word into a single reg-75
     write.
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

3. `set-bin+=<fields>` / `set-bin-=<fields>`: OR-in / mask-out
   Common fields incrementally instead of restating the whole
   set. The set-arithmetic generalizes to the other bitmask
   registers — Binary Output 2/3 (regs 76/77, identical layout)
   and reg 75's `asyncMode` serial-port mask — but not to
   `set-ascii` (reg 6 is a single-valued preset, so only
   `=on`/`=off` fits there). Builds on the orthogonal `set-bin`
   verbs. [[1]]

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 feat: decompose output config into register verbs —
     `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `set-ascii-hz` (reg 7),
     `get/set-bin` (reg 75), and `set-bin-hz` (the reg-75
     divisor), all as `key=value` tokens. Drops `get-hz`/`set-hz`.
     Keeps the legacy configure/measure `bench` as a parallel path
     until -5. (done)
   - 0.3.0-5 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. Now exercisable via the -4 verbs (`set-bin`
     then `bench`). (done — impl landed; an intermittent
     cold-open 0/0 remains, tracked as Todo #1)
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     merge `set-bin`+`set-bin-hz` in one word into a single reg-75
     write.
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Fix passive `bench` intermittent zero-parse: it sometimes
   reports `ASCII: none / Binary: none` then works on a re-run.
   The symptom varies — full ~269 kbit/s in some failures, a low
   ~24 kbit/s in another — so start by checking device state
   (`get-bin` / `get-ascii` / `rrg 5`), then capture a real
   failure via an env-gated raw dump and diff against the clean
   `test-data/both-streams.bin`. [[8]]

2. `set-bin+=<fields>` / `set-bin-=<fields>`: OR-in / mask-out
   Common fields incrementally instead of restating the whole
   set. The set-arithmetic generalizes to the other bitmask
   registers — Binary Output 2/3 (regs 76/77, identical layout)
   and reg 75's `asyncMode` serial-port mask — but not to
   `set-ascii` (reg 6 is a single-valued preset, not a bitmask).
   Builds on the orthogonal `set-bin` verbs. [[1]]

Green — fmt/clippy clean, 32 tests pass. Final commit (now includes `notes/todo.md`):

```
feat: orthogonal set-bin verbs (fields vs on/off)

Decouple the binary field mask from streaming on/off so config
writes can land on a quiet wire — set the fields with output off,
then enable. Pulled forward from the verb-redesign Todo because the
dual-stream reliability test needs a contention-free setup.

- src/lib.rs: SetBin no longer enables on a field write. Fields
  preserves async_mode, Enable (=on) sets the port. Guard =on
  against an empty mask (would stream a device-rejected zero-field
  frame). The rate-defaulted note now fires on the field/enable
  paths.
- src/cli.rs
  - add `set-bin=on` parsing
  - make bare `set-bin` an error (enable is explicit now, like
    bare set-ascii)
  - help row → `set-bin=<FIELDS|on|off>`, plus configure→rate→on
    examples
  - update the BinSet doc and the parse test
- notes/todo.md
  - close the orthogonal-verbs Todo to Done (binary done, ASCII
    N/A — reg 6 has no separate enable bit)
  - renumber the `+=`/`-=` entry to #2
- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version-of-record 0.3.0-5.1 -> 0.3.0-5.2.
```

Five files: `src/lib.rs`, `src/cli.rs`, `notes/todo.md`, `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`. `ochid:` injected by the wrapper, target `main`, keep-separate.

Ready to push on your word.

message.content:
This:

  set-bin=<FIELDS|on|off> Set the binary field mask, leaving streaming as-is.
                          `on` enables (needs fields), `off` disables.
                          Fields: time, ypr, quat, gyro, accel, imu, magpres

Reads like on and off control how the listed fields changed,
but I think the FIELDS replaces any current setting for the
fields and on|off enables and disables. Maybe we need:


  set-bin-fields=<FIELDS>
  set-bin=on|off

content.text:
You're right — overloading `set-bin=<FIELDS|on|off>` makes `on`/`off` read like field operations, and it muddles a verb that should do one thing. Your split is cleaner and it matches the existing `set-bin-hz` pattern, so the three reg-75 aspects each get their own verb:

- `set-bin-fields=<FIELDS>` — the mask
- `set-bin-hz=<HZ>` — the rate
- `set-bin=on|off` — streaming toggle (bare `set-bin` still an error)

Since `0.3.0-5.2` isn't pushed yet, I'll fold this into it rather than ship the ambiguous form. Let me find every `set-bin` reference to update them all consistently.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 feat: decompose output config into register verbs —
     `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `set-ascii-hz` (reg 7),
     `get/set-bin` (reg 75), and `set-bin-hz` (the reg-75
     divisor), all as `key=value` tokens. Drops `get-hz`/`set-hz`.
     Keeps the legacy configure/measure `bench` as a parallel path
     until -5. (done)
   - 0.3.0-5 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. Now exercisable via the -4 verbs (`set-bin`
     then `bench`). (done — impl landed; an intermittent
     cold-open 0/0 remains, tracked as Todo #1)
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     merge `set-bin`+`set-bin-hz` in one word into a single reg-75
     write.
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then

   (`get-bin` / `get-ascii` / `rrg 5`), then capture a real

2. `set-bin-fields+=<FIELDS>` / `set-bin-fields-=<FIELDS>`: OR-in /
   mask-out Common fields incrementally instead of restating the
   whole set. The set-arithmetic generalizes to the other bitmask
   registers — Binary Output 2/3 (regs 76/77, identical layout)
   and reg 75's `asyncMode` serial-port mask — but not to
   `set-ascii` (reg 6 is a single-valued preset, not a bitmask).
   Builds on the orthogonal `set-bin` verbs. [[1]]

## Done

attachment.snippet:
1	//! Command-line surface — argument parsing, the `Command` enum, and help.
2	//!
3	//! - `parse_args` turns argv into a `Config` (port/baud) plus a `Command`.
4	//! - `help_text` renders usage; the ASCII-type helpers map CLI names to
5	//!   register values.
6	
7	use crate::proto::*;
8	
9	/// Frequencies (Hz) the VN-100 accepts for the async data output rate.
10	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 7" (ICD §3.2.4 Table 3.9; vnsdk AsyncOutputFreq::Adof).
11	const VALID_RATES: &[u32] = &[1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200];
12	
13	/// Serial baud rates the VN-100 supports (register 5).
14	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 5" (ICD §3.2.2 Table 3.3; vnsdk BaudRate::BaudRates).
15	const VALID_BAUDS: &[u32] = &[
16	    9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600,
17	];
18	
19	/// ASCII async message presets (register 6 ADOR): CLI name -> register value.
20	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 6" (ICD §3.2.3 Table 3.6;
21	/// vnsdk AsyncOutputType::Ador).
22	const ASCII_TYPES: &[(&str, u8)] = &[
23	    ("off", 0),
24	    ("ypr", 1),
25	    ("qtn", 2),
26	    ("qmr", 8),
27	    ("mag", 10),
28	    ("acc", 11),
29	    ("gyr", 12),
30	    ("mar", 13),
31	    ("ymr", 14), // the factory default
32	    ("yba", 16),
33	    ("yia", 17),
34	    ("imu", 19),
35	    ("dtv", 30),
36	    ("hve", 34),
37	];
38	
39	fn ascii_type_names() -> String {
40	    ASCII_TYPES
41	        .iter()
42	        .map(|(n, _)| *n)
43	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
44	        .join(", ")
45	}
46	
47	/// Resolve a `--type` name (case-insensitive, optional `vn` prefix) to its ADOR.
48	fn parse_ascii_type(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
49	    let lower = s.trim().to_lowercase();
50	    let key = lower.strip_prefix("vn").unwrap_or(&lower);
51	    ASCII_TYPES
52	        .iter()
53	        .find(|(n, _)| *n == key)
54	        .map(|(_, v)| *v)
55	        .ok_or_else(|| {
56	            format!(
57	                "unknown ASCII type `{s}`; choose from {}",
58	                ascii_type_names()
59	            )
60	        })
61	}
62	
63	/// Display name for an ADOR value, e.g. 8 -> "VNYMR".
64	pub fn ascii_type_name(value: u8) -> String {
65	    ASCII_TYPES
66	        .iter()
67	        .find(|(_, v)| *v == value)
68	        .map(|(n, _)| {
69	            if *n == "off" {
70	                "off".to_string()
71	            } else {
72	                format!("VN{}", n.to_uppercase())
73	            }
74	        })
75	        .unwrap_or_else(|| format!("ADOR {value}"))
76	}
77	
78	pub struct Config {
79	    pub port: String,
80	    pub baud: u32,
81	}
82	
83	/// What a binary-output config step does (register 75).
84	///
85	/// Fields and on/off live in separate verbs so they stay orthogonal
86	/// — a mask write never toggles streaming, and a streaming toggle
87	/// never touches the mask:
88	///
89	/// - `Fields` — `set-bin-fields=<list>`: set the Common field mask
90	///   to these fields, leaving streaming on/off unchanged.
91	/// - `Enable` — `set-bin=on`: enable streaming with the device's
92	///   current mask (an error if no fields are configured).
93	/// - `Off` — `set-bin=off`: disable streaming, leaving the mask
94	///   configured.
95	pub enum BinSet {
96	    Fields(Vec<&'static Field>),
97	    Enable,
98	    Off,
99	}
100	
101	pub enum Command {
102	    Help,
103	    Version,
104	    /// Read the ASCII async preset (register 6).
105	    GetAscii,
106	    /// Write the ASCII async preset (register 6); `off` disables it.
107	    SetAscii {
108	        preset: u8,
109	        persist: bool,
110	    },
111	    /// Write the ASCII async rate (register 7).
112	    SetAsciiHz {
113	        hz: u32,
114	        persist: bool,
115	    },
116	    /// Read the binary output config (register 75).
117	    GetBin,
118	    /// Configure the binary output field mask (register 75).
119	    SetBin {
120	        action: BinSet,
121	        persist: bool,
122	    },
123	    /// Write the binary output rate as register 75's rateDivisor (`800/hz`).
124	    SetBinHz {
125	        hz: u32,
126	        persist: bool,
127	    },
128	    SetBaud {
129	        baud: u32,
130	        persist: bool,
131	    },
132	    Reset,
133	    FactoryReset,
134	    /// Read any register (generic ASCII Read Register).
135	    Rrg {
136	        id: u8,
137	    },
138	    /// Write any register (generic ASCII Write Register).
139	    Wrg {
140	        id: u8,
141	        params: Vec<String>,
142	    },
143	    /// Passively measure whatever the device is already streaming for `secs`
144	    /// seconds — no device writes. Reports ASCII async line rate, binary
145	    /// Common-group frame rate, and total wire throughput.
146	    Bench {
147	        secs: u64,
148	    },
149	}
150	
151	/// Format one help row: a `label` and one-or-more wrapped description lines,
152	/// aligned to a common description column. If the label is too wide, the
153	/// description starts on the next line.
154	fn help_row(label: &str, desc: &[&str]) -> String {
155	    const COL: usize = 24; // label column width; description starts after it
156	    let mut out = String::new();
157	    if label.len() <= COL {
158	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label:<COL$}{}\n", desc[0]));
159	    } else {
160	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label}\n"));
161	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", desc[0]));
162	    }
163	    for cont in &desc[1..] {
164	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", cont));
165	    }
166	    out
167	}
168	
169	pub fn help_text() -> String {
170	    let mut s = String::new();
171	    s.push_str("rw-vn100 - read/configure a VectorNav VN-100 over serial\n\n");
172	    s.push_str("Usage: rw-vn100 [--port PORT] [--baud BAUD] <command> [args]\n\n");
173	
174	    s.push_str("Commands:\n");
175	    s.push_str("  The output-config verbs each own one register. Adding `--persist`\n");
176	    s.push_str("  on a `set-*` causes the register to be written to flash.\n\n");
177	    s.push_str(&help_row(
178	        "get-ascii",
179	        &["Read the ASCII async preset (register 6)."],
180	    ));
181	    s.push_str(&help_row(
182	        "set-ascii=<PRESET|off>",
183	        &[
184	            "Set the ASCII async preset (register 6).",
185	            &format!("Presets: {}", ascii_type_names()),
186	        ],
187	    ));
188	    s.push_str(&help_row(
189	        "set-ascii-hz=<HZ>",
190	        &["Set the ASCII async rate (register 7)."],
191	    ));
192	    s.push_str(&help_row(
193	        "get-bin",
194	        &["Read the binary output: port, rate, fields (register 75)."],
195	    ));
196	    s.push_str(&help_row(
197	        "set-bin-fields=<FIELDS>",
198	        &[
199	            "Set the binary field mask (register 75), leaving",
200	            "streaming as-is.",
201	            &format!("Fields: {}", field_names()),
202	        ],
203	    ));
204	    s.push_str(&help_row(
205	        "set-bin-hz=<HZ>",
206	        &["Set the binary rate (register 75 divisor; HZ must divide 800)."],
207	    ));
208	    s.push_str(&help_row(
209	        "set-bin=on|off",
210	        &["Enable or disable binary streaming (needs fields to enable)."],
211	    ));
212	    s.push_str(&help_row(
213	        "baud <NEW> [--persist]",
214	        &[
215	            "Change serial baud (register 5); switch this",
216	            "connection to it and verify, without closing",
217	            "the port.",
218	        ],
219	    ));
220	    s.push_str(&help_row(
221	        "rrg <ID>",
222	        &["Read any register; print its fields."],
223	    ));
224	    s.push_str(&help_row(
225	        "wrg <ID> <P1> [P2...]",
226	        &[
227	            "Write any register. Sharp tool: e.g.",
228	            "`wrg 5 921600` skips the safe baud switch —",
229	            "use `baud` instead.",
230	        ],
231	    ));
232	    s.push_str(&help_row(
233	        "bench [SECS]",
234	        &[
235	            "Passively measure whatever is already streaming",
236	            "(default 5 s) — no device writes. Reports ASCII",
237	            "line rate, binary frame rate, and wire throughput.",
238	        ],
239	    ));
240	    s.push_str(&help_row(
241	        "reset | factory-reset",

content.text:
Everything reads cleanly now — each verb is unambiguous, and `set-bin=time,accel` gives a helpful redirect to `set-bin-fields`. (The exit-2 is just the error-path commands; expected.)

Final commit, retitled to reflect the split:

```
feat: split set-bin into fields / on-off verbs

Give each reg-75 aspect its own verb so none is overloaded:
set-bin-fields sets the mask, set-bin-hz the rate, set-bin=on|off
the streaming toggle. The old set-bin=<FIELDS|on|off> conflated
mask edits with the on/off toggle. Decoupling also lets config
writes land on a quiet wire (set fields with output off, then
enable), which the dual-stream reliability test needs.

- src/lib.rs
  - SetBin no longer enables on a field write: Fields preserves
    async_mode, Enable (=on) sets the port
  - guard =on against an empty mask (a zero-field frame the device
    rejects)
  - the rate-defaulted note now fires on the field/enable paths
  - module-doc and error strings name the new verbs
- src/cli.rs
  - add `set-bin-fields=<FIELDS>` and restrict `set-bin` to on/off
    (bare or other value errors)
  - update help rows, examples, command list, BinSet doc, tests
- notes/todo.md
  - close the verb-split Todo to Done (binary done, ASCII N/A —
    reg 6 has no separate enable bit)
  - rename the increment Todo to `set-bin-fields+=`/`-=`
- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version-of-record 0.3.0-5.1 -> 0.3.0-5.2.
```

Five files: `src/lib.rs`, `src/cli.rs`, `notes/todo.md`, `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`. `ochid:` injected by the wrapper, target `main`, keep-separate.

Ready to push on your word.

lastPrompt: Now let's run the dual-stream test at 115200

content.text:
Dual stream is configured — the device is now sending **both** ASCII VNYPR @ 40 Hz **and** binary (time, gyro, accel) @ 40 Hz on port 2. The whole config ran clean: the field-mask write landed with everything silent, and enabling didn't disturb the mask.

Go ahead and run `bench 2` and paste it. Expected:

- **ASCII:** ~40 Hz VNYPR, ~1,520 B/s
- **Binary:** ~40 Hz frames, 38 B each (time 8 + gyro 12 + accel 12 + 6 overhead), ~1,520 B/s, Common time/gyro/accel
- **Wire throughput** ~30 kbit/s ≈ 26% of the 115.2 link — lots of headroom, so any `none seen` is the cold-open bug, not saturation.

Binary: 79 frames in 2.00s = 39.5 Hz (1500 B/s).
  Fields: Common["time", "gyro", "accel"] (38 B/frame).
  Sample: t=10179550006000 ns, gyro=[-0.0010, -0.0001, 0.0012] rad/s, accel=[8.994, -2.605, -0.075] m/s^2
Wire throughput ~30 kbit/s = 26% of the 115.2 kbit/s 115200-baud link.

content.text:
Dual stream confirmed at 115200, and the numbers land right on prediction:

- **ASCII:** 40.0 Hz VNYPR, 1519 B/s (est. ~1,520)
- **Binary:** 39.5 Hz, 38 B/frame, 1500 B/s, Common time/gyro/accel
- **Total:** ~30 kbit/s = 26% link — exactly as budgeted

And the binary decode is sane: gyro ≈ 0 (stationary), accel `[8.994, -2.605, -0.075]` ≈ gravity projected onto a heavily tilted frame (YPR pitch +74°, roll +88°) — consistent with the ASCII attitude. So the orthogonal-verb flow produced a clean, correct dual stream with the field write landing on a silent wire.

**But** — this is one *warm* run, so it's a config success, not a reproduction of the intermittent zero-parse. That bug bites *fresh-process opens* ("works on a re-run"), so the way to provoke it is many back-to-back fresh `bench` invocations. Two ways forward:

1. **Probe first (cheap, no code):** run a loop of fresh benches and count any `none seen` to confirm it still reproduces in this dual-stream config and get a rough rate. One-liner you can run and paste:
   ```
   for i in $(seq 1 20); do echo -n "run $i: "; rw-vn100 bench 1 --baud 115200 2>&1 | grep -E "ASCII:|Binary:" | tr '\n' ' '; echo; done
   ```
2. **Instrument first:** build the in-memory capture mode (the Todo #1 env-gated raw dump) *before* probing — so the moment a run fails, we've captured the bytes to diff against `test-data/both-streams.bin`. A transient failure with no capture is a wasted reproduction.

My rec: run the probe loop now to confirm it reproduces (and how often). If it fires even once in 20, we build the capture mode and re-probe with it armed. If 20/20 are clean, we rethink — maybe it needs cold power-cycle opens (persist the config + power-cycle), which is a different setup.

Want to run that loop and paste, or should I run it here?

content.text:
The new line validates cleanly:

- ASCII 1513 B/s + Binary 1513 B/s = **3026 B/s** parsed
- Wire total = **3035 B/s**
- Gap = **9 B/s** (~18 bytes over 2.01s)

That ~0.3% gap is just measurement-boundary partials — a line or frame straddling the start/stop of the 2s window, counted in the raw byte total but not completed into a message/frame. So the wire is **clean**: essentially every byte is accounted for as ASCII or binary, no junk. Exactly the validation you wanted, and a healthy signal. (Binary decode still sane too — gyro ≈ 0, accel ≈ gravity on the tilted frame.)

This is a third clean run, though — dual stream is solid but the intermittent zero-parse hasn't shown. Time to actually provoke it with back-to-back fresh opens. Here's the probe loop again (now each run also shows the B/s gap, so a partial-stream failure is obvious):

```
for i in $(seq 1 20); do echo -n "run $i: "; rw-vn100 bench 1 --baud 115200 2>&1 | grep -E "ASCII:|Binary:|Wire" | tr '\n' ' '; echo; done
```

Run that and paste — we're watching for any run that shows `ASCII: none` or `Binary: none` (or a throughput gap much bigger than a few B/s). If even one of 20 trips, that's our reproduction and we build the in-memory capture next. Want to run it, or should I?

content.text:
**20/20 clean** — every run shows both streams at 40 Hz, no `none seen`, throughput steady. So the zero-parse did **not** reproduce in this config.

Reading the noise:
- The B/s split into two clusters (~1519 vs ~1494) is a **window-quantization artifact**, not a real rate change: each run captures exactly 40 messages, but elapsed time is 1.00 s vs 1.02 s, so dividing by the longer window gives the lower B/s. The device is rock-steady at 40 Hz.
- The wire gap is consistently ~15–16 B/s (one partial line/frame at the 1 s boundary). Clean every time.

**The telling clue is in Todo #1's own data:** the past failures cited "~269 kbit/s" throughput. That's impossible on a 115200 link (max ~115 kbit/s) — so *those failures happened at a higher baud* (269 kbit/s ≈ 29% of 921600). We think the intermittent zero-parse is tied to **higher baud / misframing** (the same family as Bug #1's RS-232 high-baud wedge), not a 115200 defect. That's consistent with 20/20 clean here.

But we've only tested **40 Hz** binary — and the fc.py goal is **200 Hz**. That's the config that actually matters, and at 5× the frame rate it stresses the parser far more. It still fits 115200: ypr ASCII (~1,520 B/s) + binary `time,gyro,accel` @200 Hz (38 B × 200 = 7,600 B/s) = ~9,120 B/s ≈ **79%** of the link. Same reliable baud, no wedge risk.

So I'd **bump binary to 200 Hz and re-run the 20× probe** — that's the meaningful reliability test for the goal. I'd do it as a quiet rate change (silence ASCII → `set-bin=off` → `set-bin-hz=200` → `set-bin=on` → `set-ascii=ypr`), then loop. If 200 Hz is also clean, 115200 is validated for the 200 Hz path; if it trips, that's the reproduction that matters.

Want me to bump to 200 Hz and re-probe?

content.text:
**20/20 clean at 200 Hz — 40/40 across both probes, zero failures.** The zero-parse did not reproduce even at the full fc.py target rate and 79% link load.

Validation (the new B/s line earning its keep):
- Run 1: ASCII 1519 + binary 7594 = 9113 parsed vs 9119 wire → **6 B/s gap**. Every run's gap stays in the 6–16 B/s range — clean wire even at 79% utilization, no unparsed junk.
- The occasional 199-frame or 39-message run (e.g. run 18) is just the 1 s window catching one fewer at the boundary — the device holds 200.0 / 40.0 Hz steady.

**Conclusion:** binary 200 Hz + ASCII alongside at **115200 is reliable** — 40 fresh opens, 0 drops. That's the fc.py target config validated on the proven baud. And the intermittent zero-parse is **not a 115200 phenomenon**: we couldn't provoke it in 40 tries, and Todo #1's own "~269 kbit/s" failure can't exist on a 115200 link (max ~115 kbit/s), so those failures were at a higher baud — we think it's the same high-baud misframing family as Bug #1, not a distinct bug here.

So the device side is in good shape for the 200 Hz goal. Where to next:

1. **Record the finding** — update Todo #1 with this evidence (40/40 clean at 115200 incl. 200 Hz; symptom appears baud-dependent, likely overlapping Bug #1). Small notes commit. I'd recommend this regardless of what's next, so the result isn't lost.
2. **Move toward the fc.py 200 Hz patch** — the device path is now proven, so this is the natural next goal.
3. **Chase the bug at 921600** — only if you want to confirm the high-baud hypothesis, but that reintroduces the wedge risk and is off the reliable path.

My rec: do (1) now, then (2). Note the device is currently left at 200 Hz binary + ypr ASCII (volatile — a power cycle reverts to flash default ymr/115200). How do you want to proceed?

message.content:
Record the finding in Todo #1, but now we need to incrementally bump baud towards 921600
which is where we saw are failures, IIRC. I'm suggesting incrementally so we can
possibly identify the cross over point. Thoughts?

content.text:
Agreed — incremental is the right way to find the crossover, and there's a sharper framing that makes this experiment doubly worth it: **Bug #1's wedge was only ever seen on the RS-232 rig, and we noted "we think it may not reproduce on TTL."** We're on the TTL header now (`/dev/ttyAMA0`). So this sweep simultaneously hunts the zero-parse crossover *and* tests the TTL-immunity hypothesis. Two outcomes, both valuable:
- Clean all the way to 921600 → the wedge/misframe was RS-232-specific, and 921600 is usable on TTL (great news for headroom).
- Breaks at some baud → we've found the TTL crossover.

**Finding I'll record in Todo #1** (for your review before it lands):

> *Finding (2026-06-28):* 40/40 fresh-open benches clean at 115200 dual-stream (ASCII ypr 40 Hz + binary 200 Hz, 79% link) — the zero-parse does not reproduce at 115200. The cited ~269 kbit/s failure can't occur on a 115200 link (max ~115 kbit/s), so those failures were at a higher baud. We think this is the same high-baud misframing family as Bug #1, not a 115200 defect. Next: sweep baud 115200 → 230400 → 460800 → 921600 to find the crossover (also tests whether the wedge reproduces on TTL).

**Experiment mechanics** (a couple matter):
- The **in-session `baud` switch is clean** at every baud (per Bug #1) — it's the **fresh-process bench reopen** that wedges. So per level: `baud <next>` to switch the device, then N fresh `bench` reopens (that's the part under test). Keep the dual stream on (realistic; at higher baud it's a smaller % so easy).
- **Baud stays volatile — never `--persist`.** That's the safety net: any wedge recovers with a power cycle back to flash-default 115200 (you're at the rig). A persisted high baud could brick the link.
- **Classify each failure:** a *transient* zero-parse (re-run recovers) is the Todo #1 symptom; *persistent* silence at every baud is a Bug #1 wedge → stop and power-cycle. On the first failure I'll re-test before concluding.
- A power cycle resets our volatile config, so we'd reconfigure the dual stream after one.

**One choice before I start** — how to sweep:
- **Chained (no power cycles):** bump 230400 → 460800 → 921600 in one pass, ~10 reopens each. Fast, aggressive (cumulative stress — Bug #1 wedged at 230400 this way). Good for a quick "does TTL break at all."
- **Power-cycle-isolated:** clean power cycle before each baud, so each level's intrinsic rate is measured (Bug #1's controlled retest stayed clean to 921600 minus the top). Cleaner crossover, but you power-cycle between levels.

My rec: **chained first** (fast, finds if/where TTL breaks), then isolate around the crossover only if it breaks. Sound good, and ~10 reopens per level? Once you confirm, I'll record the finding and start the sweep.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 feat: decompose output config into register verbs —
     `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `set-ascii-hz` (reg 7),
     `get/set-bin` (reg 75), and `set-bin-hz` (the reg-75
     divisor), all as `key=value` tokens. Drops `get-hz`/`set-hz`.
     Keeps the legacy configure/measure `bench` as a parallel path
     until -5. (done)
   - 0.3.0-5 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. Now exercisable via the -4 verbs (`set-bin`
     then `bench`). (done — impl landed; an intermittent
     cold-open 0/0 remains, tracked as Todo #1)
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     merge `set-bin`+`set-bin-hz` in one word into a single reg-75
     write.
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Fix passive `bench` intermittent zero-parse: it sometimes
   reports `ASCII: none / Binary: none` then works on a re-run.
   The symptom varies — full ~269 kbit/s in some failures, a low
   ~24 kbit/s in another — so start by checking device state
   (`get-bin` / `get-ascii` / `rrg 5`), then capture a real
   failure via an env-gated raw dump and diff against the clean
   `test-data/both-streams.bin`. [[8]]

2. `set-bin-fields+=<FIELDS>` / `set-bin-fields-=<FIELDS>`: OR-in /
   mask-out Common fields incrementally instead of restating the
   whole set. The set-arithmetic generalizes to the other bitmask
   registers — Binary Output 2/3 (regs 76/77, identical layout)
   and reg 75's `asyncMode` serial-port mask — but not to
   `set-ascii` (reg 6 is a single-valued preset, not a bitmask).
   Builds on the orthogonal `set-bin` verbs. [[1]]

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: default RPi5 UART, fix binary port on TTL [[2]],[[3]]
- fix: bench silences async before binary config [[4]]
- feat: decompose output config into register verbs [[6]]
- feat: passive bench measures the live stream [[7]]
- feat: split set-bin into fields / on-off verbs — `set-bin-fields`
  sets the mask, `set-bin=on`/`off` toggles streaming, keeping them
  orthogonal. ASCII stays as-is: reg 6 (ADOR) has no separate enable
  bit (0=off, N=preset), so it can't be orthogonal without stateful
  preset memory. [[1]]

attachment.prompt: Do we want to do -6, the "step grammar", to give finer control?

lastPrompt: The failures we saw yesterday were on the rpi5 just as we are now. I vote no pwer cycles to start, as my guess it's more likely to fail.

content.text:
Good question, but my vote is **not now** — and the reason is precise: the bug we're hunting *is* the fresh-process reopen. Separate `rw-vn100` invocations are exactly what trips it ("works on a re-run"), so a stream of independent `bench` opens is the right instrument. The `-6` step grammar's whole point is a **single** port open — running config + bench over one connection — which would *eliminate* the reopens and thus mask the very thing we're trying to provoke. For the hunt, we want the die-rolls, not fewer of them.

Two more reasons it's not needed right now:

- **We can already isolate reopen-vs-in-session without it.** The `baud` command switches *and verifies on the live connection* — that's an in-session read at the new baud (the path Bug #1 found clean). The subsequent fresh `bench --baud N` is the reopen (the suspect path). So if the switch+verify succeeds but the next `bench` fails, we've attributed it to the reopen — with the tools we have.
- **`-6` is a substantial feature** (shell-word steps, `+` join, single open, left-to-right). Building it now interrupts a live reproduction attempt to write code that would obscure the symptom.

Where `-6` *does* fit: it's the eventual **mitigation** — "each reopen is a wedge die-roll," so one connection = fewer die-rolls — and a sharper single-connection diagnostic *after* we know the crossover (e.g. `baud 921600 + bench` in one open vs reopen, to confirm the reopen is the TTL trigger). So I'd build it once we've characterized the bug, as the planned next step.

So: finish the chained sweep first with the current fresh-reopen probes. I've recorded the finding in Todo #1 (uncommitted) and I'm paused right before switching to 230400. Proceed with `baud 230400` + 10× probe, or do you want to pivot to `-6` first?

message.content: Do we want to add in-memory data capture, I think so, but you're welcome to persuade me otherwise.

content.text:
You've basically persuaded me — but let me give you the honest counter-case first, then say why I land on yes.

**The counter (sweep-first):** we don't *know* the sweep reproduces yet, so we could sweep with the current probes, and only build capture once we've confirmed a failure baud — avoiding building an instrument we might not need. There's also a real limit: if the TTL failure turns out to be a *wedge* (silent), capture grabs nothing — empty buffer, no bytes to analyze. Capture only earns its keep on the *misframe* case (bytes arrive but don't parse).

**Why yes anyway:**
- Yesterday's failures were the **~269 kbit/s "none seen"** signature — that's misframe (bytes arriving), not silent wedge. So capture is squarely aimed at the failure mode we actually saw.
- The failure is **intermittent and precious**. If it trips during the sweep with no capture armed, we've burned a reproduction to learn only "it failed at baud N" — not *how* the bytes are mangled. Re-reproducing an intermittent bug to get the bytes is the expensive path.
- It's the **instrument Todo #1 already prescribes** ("capture a real failure via an env-gated raw dump and diff against `test-data/both-streams.bin`"). Unlike `-6`, it doesn't mask the bug — it's passive observation, and **byte-for-byte unchanged when the env var is unset**, so it can't perturb the experiment.
- It's **small and cheap**: a few lines in the bench read loop, in-memory `Vec<u8>`, RAM trivial (a 2 s capture is tens of KB even at 921600).

So: agree, build it first, then sweep with it armed.

**Proposed scope (`0.3.0-5.4`):**
- `RW_VN100_CAPTURE=<path>` env var. When set, `bench` appends every byte it reads off the wire into a `Vec<u8>` and writes it to `<path>` at the end of the run. Unset → no behavior change.
- Raw bytes, so it diffs directly against `test-data/both-streams.bin` (which is the same format).
- Scoped to `bench` (the passive dual-stream read — where the symptom lives).
- I'd fold the already-edited Todo #1 finding into this commit, since the instrument and the finding are the same investigation.

One open choice: **raw bytes only** (MVP, directly diffable) vs **also a sidecar of per-read timestamps/lengths** (lets us see *when* in the first second it goes wrong — useful for a reopen-timing bug, but more scope). I'd ship raw-bytes first and add timing only if the raw diff doesn't explain it.

content.text: Good call — pre-sizing keeps the read loop allocation-free, which also avoids perturbing timing during the capture (relevant when we're chasing a timing-sensitive bug). And we don't need a parameter: the wire has a hard physical ceiling — `baud/10` bytes/sec (8N1 = 10 bits/byte) — so `(baud/10) × secs` is a guaranteed upper bound. `bench` already has both, so I'll compute it there and pre-size the sink to exactly the link max (+ one read buffer for the final partial read past the deadline). That means zero reallocations, ever.

1	//! Command-line surface — argument parsing, the `Command` enum, and help.
2	//!
3	//! - `parse_args` turns argv into a `Config` (port/baud) plus a `Command`.
4	//! - `help_text` renders usage; the ASCII-type helpers map CLI names to
5	//!   register values.
6	
7	use crate::proto::*;
8	
9	/// Frequencies (Hz) the VN-100 accepts for the async data output rate.
10	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 7" (ICD §3.2.4 Table 3.9; vnsdk AsyncOutputFreq::Adof).
11	const VALID_RATES: &[u32] = &[1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200];
12	
13	/// Serial baud rates the VN-100 supports (register 5).
14	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 5" (ICD §3.2.2 Table 3.3; vnsdk BaudRate::BaudRates).
15	const VALID_BAUDS: &[u32] = &[
16	    9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600,
17	];
18	
19	/// ASCII async message presets (register 6 ADOR): CLI name -> register value.
20	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 6" (ICD §3.2.3 Table 3.6;
21	/// vnsdk AsyncOutputType::Ador).
22	const ASCII_TYPES: &[(&str, u8)] = &[
23	    ("off", 0),
24	    ("ypr", 1),
25	    ("qtn", 2),
26	    ("qmr", 8),
27	    ("mag", 10),
28	    ("acc", 11),
29	    ("gyr", 12),
30	    ("mar", 13),
31	    ("ymr", 14), // the factory default
32	    ("yba", 16),
33	    ("yia", 17),
34	    ("imu", 19),
35	    ("dtv", 30),
36	    ("hve", 34),
37	];
38	
39	fn ascii_type_names() -> String {
40	    ASCII_TYPES
41	        .iter()
42	        .map(|(n, _)| *n)
43	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
44	        .join(", ")
45	}
46	
47	/// Resolve a `--type` name (case-insensitive, optional `vn` prefix) to its ADOR.
48	fn parse_ascii_type(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
49	    let lower = s.trim().to_lowercase();
50	    let key = lower.strip_prefix("vn").unwrap_or(&lower);
51	    ASCII_TYPES
52	        .iter()
53	        .find(|(n, _)| *n == key)
54	        .map(|(_, v)| *v)
55	        .ok_or_else(|| {
56	            format!(
57	                "unknown ASCII type `{s}`; choose from {}",
58	                ascii_type_names()
59	            )
60	        })
61	}
62	
63	/// Display name for an ADOR value, e.g. 8 -> "VNYMR".
64	pub fn ascii_type_name(value: u8) -> String {
65	    ASCII_TYPES
66	        .iter()
67	        .find(|(_, v)| *v == value)
68	        .map(|(n, _)| {
69	            if *n == "off" {
70	                "off".to_string()
71	            } else {
72	                format!("VN{}", n.to_uppercase())
73	            }
74	        })
75	        .unwrap_or_else(|| format!("ADOR {value}"))
76	}
77	
78	pub struct Config {
79	    pub port: String,
80	    pub baud: u32,
81	}
82	
83	/// What a binary-output config step does (register 75).
84	///
85	/// Fields and on/off live in separate verbs so they stay orthogonal
86	/// — a mask write never toggles streaming, and a streaming toggle
87	/// never touches the mask:
88	///
89	/// - `Fields` — `set-bin-fields=<list>`: set the Common field mask
90	///   to these fields, leaving streaming on/off unchanged.
91	/// - `Enable` — `set-bin=on`: enable streaming with the device's
92	///   current mask (an error if no fields are configured).
93	/// - `Off` — `set-bin=off`: disable streaming, leaving the mask
94	///   configured.
95	pub enum BinSet {
96	    Fields(Vec<&'static Field>),
97	    Enable,
98	    Off,
99	}
100	
101	pub enum Command {
102	    Help,
103	    Version,
104	    /// Read the ASCII async preset (register 6).
105	    GetAscii,
106	    /// Write the ASCII async preset (register 6); `off` disables it.
107	    SetAscii {
108	        preset: u8,
109	        persist: bool,
110	    },
111	    /// Write the ASCII async rate (register 7).
112	    SetAsciiHz {
113	        hz: u32,
114	        persist: bool,
115	    },
116	    /// Read the binary output config (register 75).
117	    GetBin,
118	    /// Configure the binary output field mask (register 75).
119	    SetBin {
120	        action: BinSet,
121	        persist: bool,
122	    },
123	    /// Write the binary output rate as register 75's rateDivisor (`800/hz`).
124	    SetBinHz {
125	        hz: u32,
126	        persist: bool,
127	    },
128	    SetBaud {
129	        baud: u32,
130	        persist: bool,
131	    },
132	    Reset,
133	    FactoryReset,
134	    /// Read any register (generic ASCII Read Register).
135	    Rrg {
136	        id: u8,
137	    },
138	    /// Write any register (generic ASCII Write Register).
139	    Wrg {
140	        id: u8,
141	        params: Vec<String>,
142	    },
143	    /// Passively measure whatever the device is already streaming for `secs`
144	    /// seconds — no device writes. Reports ASCII async line rate, binary
145	    /// Common-group frame rate, and total wire throughput.
146	    Bench {
147	        secs: u64,
148	        /// Raw-capture path from `--capture`; dumps every wire byte read.
149	        capture: Option<String>,
150	    },
151	}
152	
153	/// Format one help row: a `label` and one-or-more wrapped description lines,
154	/// aligned to a common description column. If the label is too wide, the
155	/// description starts on the next line.
156	fn help_row(label: &str, desc: &[&str]) -> String {
157	    const COL: usize = 24; // label column width; description starts after it
158	    let mut out = String::new();
159	    if label.len() <= COL {
160	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label:<COL$}{}\n", desc[0]));
161	    } else {
162	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label}\n"));
163	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", desc[0]));
164	    }
165	    for cont in &desc[1..] {
166	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", cont));
167	    }
168	    out
169	}
170

content.text:
Both work: `bench --capture <path>` dumped 9126 bytes (matches throughput), and `get-bin --capture` is rejected with *"--capture applies to `bench`, not other verbs"*. The flag is more discoverable than the env var and shows in `--help`.

Updated commit (`0.3.0-5.4`):

```
feat: bench raw-byte capture (--capture <path>)

Add a `--capture <path>` flag so an intermittent zero-parse can be
caught and diffed against a known-good stream — the instrument
Todo #1 prescribes, ahead of the baud-crossover sweep.

- src/bench.rs
  - `CaptureReader` Read adapter records every byte read, and
    `bench` writes the buffer to the `--capture` path at exit
  - sink preallocated to the link ceiling (baud/10 × secs) so the
    read loop never reallocates
  - no path → read path byte-for-byte unchanged
  - test: capture records every byte of an interleaved stream
- src/cli.rs
  - `--capture <path>` global flag on `Command::Bench`, rejected
    for any verb but `bench`
  - help: bench row note + Global options entry
  - test: `--capture` parsing and the bench-only rejection
- src/lib.rs: pass the `--capture` path through to `bench`
- notes/todo.md: record the Todo #1 finding (40/40 clean at 115200,
  higher-baud symptom, sweep next)
- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version-of-record 0.3.0-5.3 -> 0.3.0-5.4.
```

Six files: `src/bench.rs`, `src/cli.rs`, `src/lib.rs`, `notes/todo.md`, `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`.

One unrelated thing I noticed while in the examples block: line ~288 still has a stale `rw-vn100 bench --bin --hz 200 --fields accel,gyro` example referencing flags removed in the passive-bench redesign — broken in `--help`. I left it alone to keep this commit focused, but flag it for a quick cleanup. Push this on your word.

content.text:
`--capture` confirmed working end-to-end:

- **9136 bytes** written to `./tmp/capture.bin`, matching the wire throughput.
- It's the raw stream — note this dump *starts mid-frame* (bytes `30 c8 82 a0…` are the tail of a frame already in flight when bench opened), with the first full `fa 01 21 01` frame at offset 0x12. That's correct and expected: the capture grabs whatever's on the wire from the instant the port opens, and the scanner finds sync from there. So a failure capture will faithfully show the raw bytes, partial frames and all.
- Stream's still clean this run (40 Hz ASCII + 199 binary frames).

So the instrument is verified. Two small housekeeping notes before the sweep:
- I'd suggest adding `tmp/` to `.gitignore` so capture artifacts don't show up as untracked / risk being committed. Quick one-line change — want me to?
- For the sweep, I'll point `--capture` at a per-baud filename (e.g. `./tmp/cap-230400-run3.bin`) so a failure capture is unambiguous and we keep the clean 115200 one as the baseline to diff against.

Ready to start the chained sweep (230400 → 460800 → 921600), or add the `.gitignore` line first?

content.text:
**460800: 10/10 clean** too — all fresh reopens held, and the ms timestamps are working (`114241066` = 11:42:41.066, all unique). Notable asymmetry: the marginality showed up in the *switch* transact (one retry, a command+reply round-trip) but not in the *passive bench reopens* — consistent with reads being more forgiving than transacts.

So 230400 and 460800 are both solid on TTL. **921600 is the danger zone** — it's where Bug #1 wedged even with power cycles (on RS-232). The in-session switch should survive (maybe with retries); the fresh-reopen probe is where a wedge would hit, and that needs a physical power cycle to recover. Proceeding with the switch to 921600:

content.text:
**921600: 10/10 clean. Full sweep 30/30, no wedge, no zero-parse, no power cycles.** TTL held at every baud — 230400, 460800, and 921600 — with this config. That's a strong result on its own: the RS-232 wedge does **not** reproduce on TTL.

But here's the catch, and I think it's the real lead — **we under-loaded the test by ~3×.** Our config is 3-field binary (38 B/frame) + VNYPR = **~91 kbit/s**. Todo #1's failures were **~269 kbit/s**. Reverse-engineering that number:

- Full 7-field Common binary = 110 B/frame (the `0x0739` set, same as `test-data/both-streams.bin`). At 200 Hz = 22,000 B/s.
- VNYMR ASCII (~121 B) at 40 Hz = 4,840 B/s.
- **Total ≈ 26,840 B/s = 268 kbit/s** — almost exactly the "~269 kbit/s" failure.

So yesterday's failing config was almost certainly the **full 7-field binary + VNYMR**, not our lean one. And that matters doubly: **268 kbit/s can't fit under ~300k baud**, so that config *forces* baud ≥ 460800 — which is exactly why the failures were "at higher baud." It wasn't baud alone; it was a heavy config that only runs at high baud.

We think the bug is **data-rate dependent**, and we tested too light. To actually provoke it, match yesterday's load:

- `set-bin-fields=time,ypr,quat,gyro,accel,imu,magpres` (full, 110 B/frame)
- `set-ascii=ymr` (heavier ASCII)
- keep 200 Hz binary, stay at **921600** (268 kbit/s = 29% link there)
- re-probe 10× with capture armed

Device is currently at 921600 (volatile). Want me to reconfigure to the heavy 7-field + YMR stream and re-probe at 921600? That's the faithful reproduction of the ~269 kbit/s condition.
